 the guest at Diplow feel more curiosity than their host . how be it that nothing more be hear of Miss Harleth ? be it credible that she have refuse Mr. Grandcourt ?  this be how it happen that Grandcourt arrive at the Czarina on the fifth day after Gwendolen have leave Leubronn , and find there his uncle , Sir Hugo Mallinger , with his family , include Deronda . it be not necessarily a pleasure either to the reign power or the heir presumptive when their separate affair a touch of gout , say , in the one , and a touch of willfulness in the other happen to bring they to the same spot . Sir Hugo be an easy - temper man , tolerant both of difference and defect ; but a point of view different from his own concern the settlement of the family estate fret he rather more than if it have concern Church discipline or the ballot , and fault be the less venial for belong to a person whose existence be inconvenient to he . in no case could Grandcourt have be a nephew after his own heart ; but as the presumptive heir to the Mallinger estate he be the sign and embodiment of a chief grievance in the baronet 's life the want of a son to inherit the land , in no portion of which have he himself more than a life - interest . for in the ill - advise settlement which his father , Sir Francis , have choose to make by will , even diplow with its modicum of land have be leave under the same condition as the ancient and wide inheritance of the two Toppings Diplow , where Sir Hugo have live and hunt through many a season in his young year , and where his wife and daughter ought to have be able to retire after his death . 

 this grievance have naturally gather emphasis as the year advance , and Lady Mallinger , after have have three daughter in quick succession , have remain for eight year till now that she be over forty without produce so much as another girl ; while Sir Hugo , almost twenty year old , be at a time of life when , notwithstanding the fashionable retardation of most thing from dinner to marriage , a man 's hopefulness be apt to show sign of wear , until restore by second childhood .

 CHAPTER XVI . 

 man , like planet , have both a visible and an invisible history . the astronomer thread the darkness with strict deduction , account so for every visible arc in the wanderer 's orbit ; and the narrator of human action , if he do his work with the same completeness , would have to thread the hidden pathway of feeling and think which lead up to every moment of action , and to those moment of intense suffering which take the quality of action like the cry of Prometheus , whose chain anguish seem a great energy than the sea and sky he invoke and the deity he defy . 

 Deronda 's circumstance , indeed , have be exceptional . one moment have be burn into his life as its chief epoch a moment full of July sunshine and large pink rose shed their last petal on a grassy court enclose on three side by a gothic cloister . imagine he in such a scene : a boy of thirteen , stretch prone on the grass where it be in shadow , his curly head prop on his arm over a book , while his tutor , also read , sit on a camp - stool under shelter .  " their own child be call nephew . "

 " it be just for the propriety of the thing ; because , as you know very well , priest do not   marry , and the child be illegitimate . "

 he have always call Sir Hugo Mallinger his uncle , and when it once occur to he to ask about his father and mother , the baronet have answer , " you lose your father and mother when you be quite a little one ; that be why I take care of you . " Daniel then strain to discern something in that early twilight , have a dim sense of have be kiss very much , and surround by thin , cloudy , scented drapery , till his finger catch in something hard , which hurt he , and he begin to cry . every other memory he have be of the little world in which he still live . and at that time he do not mind about learn more , for he be too fond of Sir Hugo to be sorry for the loss of unknown parent . life be very delightful to the lad , with an uncle who be always indulgent and cheerful a fine man in the bright noon of life , whom Daniel think absolutely perfect , and whose place be one of the fine in England , at once historical ; romantic , and home - like : a picturesque architectural outgrowth from an abbey , which have still remnant of the old monastic trunk . diplow lie in another county , and be a comparatively landless place which have come into the family from a rich lawyer on the female side who wear the perruque of the restoration ; whereas the Mallingers have the grant of Monk 's topping under Henry the Eighth , and age before have hold the neighbor land of King 's Topping , trace indeed their origin to a certain Hugues le Malingre , who come in with the Conqueror and also apparently with a sickly complexion which have be happily correct in his descendant . two row of these descendant , direct and collateral , female of the male line , and male of the female , look down in the gallery over the cloister on the nephew Daniel as he walk there : man in armor with pointed beard and arch eyebrow , pinch lady in hoop and ruff with no face to speak of ; grave - look man in black velvet and stuff hip , and fair , frighten woman hold little boy by the hand ; smile politician in magnificent perruque , and lady of the prize - animal kind , with rosebud mouth and full eyelid , accord to lely ; then a generation whose face be revise and embellish in the taste of Kneller ; and so on through refined edition of the family type in the time of Reynolds and Romney , till the line end with Sir Hugo and his young brother Henleigh . this last have marry Miss Grandcourt , and take her name along with her estate , thus make a junction between two equally old family , impale the three Saracens ' head proper and three bezant of the one with the tower and falcon argent of the other , and , as it happen , unite their high advantage in the prospect of that Henleigh Mallinger Grandcourt who be at present more of an acquaintance to we than either Sir Hugo or his nephew Daniel Deronda . 

 in Sir Hugo 's youthful portrait with roll collar and high cravat , Sir Thomas Lawrence have do justice to the agreeable alacrity of expression and sanguine temperament still to be see in the original , but have do something more than justice in slightly lengthen the nose , which be in reality short than might have be expect in a Mallinger . happily the appropriate nose of the family reappear in his young brother , and be to be see in all its refined regularity in his nephew Mallinger Grandcourt . but in the nephew Daniel Deronda the family face of various type , see on the wall of the gallery ; find no reflex . still he be handsomer than any of they , and when he be thirteen might have serve as model for any painter who want to image the most memorable of boy : you could hardly have see his face thoroughly meet your without believe that human creature have do nobly in time past , and might do more nobly in time to come . the fine childlike face have this consecrating power , and make we shudder anew at all the grossness and basely - wrought grief of the world , lest they should enter here and defile . 

 but at this moment on the grass among the rose - petal , Daniel Deronda be make a first acquaintance with those grief . a new idea have enter his mind , and be begin to change the aspect of his habitual feeling as happy careless voyager be change with the sky suddenly threaten and the thought of danger arise . he sit perfectly still with his back to the tutor , while his face express rapid inward transition . the deep blush , which have come when he first start up , gradually subside ; but his feature keep that indescribable look of subdue activity which often accompany a new mental survey of familiar fact . he have not live with other boy , and his mind show the same blending of child 's ignorance with surprising knowledge which be oftener see in bright girl . have read Shakespeare as well as a great deal of history , he could have talk with the wisdom of a bookish child about man who be bear out of wedlock and be hold unfortunate in consequence , be under disadvantage which require they to be a sort of hero if they be to work themselves up to an equal standing with their legally bear brother . but he have never bring such knowledge into any association with his own lot , which have be too easy for he ever to think about it until this moment when there have dart into his mind with the magic of quick comparison , the possibility that here be the secret of his own birth , and that the man whom he call uncle be really his father . some child , even young than Daniel , have know the first arrival of care , like an ominous irremovable guest in their tender life , on the discovery that their parent , whom they have imagine able to buy everything , be poor and in hard money trouble .  " whatever your inclination lead you to , my boy . I think it right to give you the option of the army , but you shut the door on that , and I be glad . I do not   expect you to choose just yet by - and - by , when you have look about you a little more and try your mettle among old man . the university have a good wide opening into the forum . there be prize to be win , and a bit of good fortune often give the turn to a man 's taste . from what I see and hear , I should think you can take up anything you like . you be in the deep water with your classic than I ever get into , and if you be rather sick of that swimming , Cambridge be the place where you can go into mathematic with a will , and disport yourself on the dry sand as much as you like . " Deronda would have be first - rate if he have have more ambition , " be a frequent remark about he . but how could a fellow push his way properly when he object to swop for his own advantage , knock under by choice when he be within an inch of victory , and , unlike the great clive , would rather be the calf than the butcher ? it be a mistake , however , to suppose that Deronda have not his share of ambition . we know he have suffer keenly from the belief that there be a tinge of dishonor in his lot ; but there be some case , and his be one of they , in which the sense of injury breed not the will to inflict injury and climb over they as a ladder , but a hatred of all injury . he have his flash of fierceness and could hit out upon occasion , but the occasion be not always what might have be expect . for in what relate to himself his resentful impulse have be early check by a mastering affectionateness . love have a habit of say " never mind " to angry self , who , sit down for the nonce in the low place , by - and - by get use to it . so it be that as Deronda approach manhood his feeling for Sir Hugo , while it be get more and more mixed with criticism , be gain in that sort of allowance which reconcile criticism with tenderness . the dear old beautiful home and everything within it , Lady Mallinger and her little one include , be consecrate for the youth as they have be for the boy only with a certain difference of light on the object . the altarpiece be no long miraculously perfect , paint under infallible guidance , but the human hand discern in the work be appeal to a reverent tenderness safe from the gust of discovery . certainly Deronda 's ambition , even in his spring - time , lie exceptionally aloof from conspicuous , vulgar triumph , and from other ugly form of boyish energy ; perhaps because he be early impassione by idea , and burn his fire on those height . one may spend a good deal of energy in dislike and resist what other pursue , and a boy who be fond of somebody else 's pencil - case may not be more energetic than another who be fond of give his own pencil - case away . still it be not Deronda 's disposition to escape from ugly scene ; he be more inclined to sit through they and take care of the fellow least able to take care of himself . it have help to make he popular that he be sometimes a little compromise by this apparent comradeship . for a meditative interest in learn how human misery be work as precocious in he as another sort of genius in the poet who write a Queen Mab at nineteen be so infused with kindliness that it easily pass for comradeship . enough . in many of our neighbor ' life there be much not only of error and lapse , but of a certain exquisite goodness which can never be write or even speak only divine by each of we , accord to the inward instruction of our own privacy . 

 the impression he make at Cambridge correspond to his position at Eton . every one interested in he agree that he might have take a high place if his motif have be of a more pushing sort , and if he have not , instead of regard study as instrument of success , hamper himself with the notion that they be to feed motive and opinion a notion which set he criticise method and argue against his freight and harness when he should have be use all his might to pull . in the beginning his work at the university have a new zest for he : indifferent to the continuation of Eton classical drill , he apply himself vigorously to mathematic , for which he have show an early aptitude under Mr. Fraser , and he have the delight of feel his strength in a comparatively fresh exercise of thought . that delight , and the favorable opinion of his tutor , determine he to try for a mathematical scholarship in the Easter of his second year : he wish to gratify Sir Hugo by some achievement , and the study of the high mathematic , have the grow fascination inherent in all thinking which demand intensity , be make he a more exclusive worker than he have be before . 

 but here come the old check which have be grow with his growth . he find the inward bent toward comprehension and thoroughness diverge more and more from the track mark out by the standard of examination : he feel a heightening discontent with the wear futility and enfeeble strain of a demand for excessive retention and dexterity without any insight into the principle which form the vital connection of knowledge .   Deronda 's undergraduateship occur fifteen year ago , when the perfection of our university method be not yet indisputable .   in hour when his dissatisfaction be strong upon he he reproach himself for have be attract by the conventional advantage of belong to an english university , and be tempt toward the project of ask Sir Hugo to let he quit Cambridge and pursue a more independent line of study abroad . the germ of this inclination have be already stir in his boyish love of universal history , which make he want to be at home in foreign country , and follow in imagination the travel student of the middle age . he long now to have the sort of apprenticeship to life which would not shape he too definitely , and rob he of the choice that might come from a free growth . one see that Deronda 's demerit be likely to be on the side of reflective hesitation , and this tendency be encourage by his position ; there be no need for he to get an immediate income , or to fit himself in haste for a profession ; and his sensibility to the half - know fact of his parentage make he an excuse for linger long than other in a state of social neutrality . other man , he inwardly say , have a more definite place and duty . but the project which flatter his inclination might not have go beyond the stage of ineffective brooding , if certain circumstance have not quicken it into action . 

 the circumstance arise out of an enthusiastic friendship which extend into his after - life . of the same year with himself , and occupy small room close to his , be a youth who have come as an exhibitioner from Christ 's Hospital , and have eccentricity enough for a Charles Lamb . only to look at his pinched feature and blonde hair hang over his collar remind one of pale quaint head by early german painter ; and when this faint coloring be light up by a joke , there come sudden crease about the mouth and eye which might have be mould by the soul of an aged humorist . his father , an engraver of some distinction , have be dead eleven year , and his mother have three girl to educate and maintain on a meagre annuity . Hans Meyrick he have be daringly christen after Holbein feel himself the pillar , or rather the knotted and twisted trunk , round which these feeble climbing plant must cling . there be no want of ability or of honest well - mean affection to make the prop trustworthy : the ease and quickness with which he study might serve he to win prize at Cambridge , as he have do among the Blue Coats , in spite of irregularity . the only danger be , that the incalculable tendency in he might be fatally time , and that his good intention might be frustrate by some act which be not due to habit but to capricious , scattered impulse . he could not be say to have any one bad habit ; yet at long or short interval he have fit of impish recklessness , and do thing that would have make the bad habit . 

 han in his right mind , however , be a lovable creature , and in Deronda he have happen to find a friend who be likely to stand by he with the more constancy , from compassion for these brief aberration that might bring a long repentance . han , indeed , share Deronda 's room nearly as much as he use his own : to Deronda he pour himself out on his study , his affair , his hope ; the poverty of his home , and his love for the creature there ; the itching of his finger to draw , and his determination to fight it away for the sake of get some sort of a plum that he might divide with his mother and the girl . he want no confidence in return , but seem to take Deronda as an Olympian who need nothing an egotism in friendship which be common enough with mercurial , expansive nature . Meyrick be go in for a classical scholarship , and his success , in various way momentous , be the more probable from the steady influence of Deronda 's friendship . 

 but an imprudence of Meyrick 's , commit at the beginning of the autumn term , threaten to disappoint his hope . with his usual alternation between unnecessary expense and self - privation , he have give too much money for an old engraving which fascinate he , and to make up for it , have come from London in a third - class carriage with his eye expose to a bitter wind and any irritate particle the wind might drive before it . the consequence be a severe inflammation of the eye , which for some time hang over he the threat of a lasting injury . this crush trouble call out all Deronda 's readiness to devote himself , and he make every other occupation secondary to that of be companion and eye to Hans , work with he and for he at his classic , that if possible his chance of the classical scholarship might be save . han , to keep the knowledge of his suffering from his mother and sister , allege his work as a reason for pass the Christmas at Cambridge , and his friend stay up with he . 

 meanwhile Deronda relax his hold on his mathematic , and Hans , reflect on this , at length say : " old fellow , while you be hoist I you be risk yourself . with your mathematical cram one may be like Moses or Mahomet or somebody of that sort who have to cram , and forget in one day what it have take he forty to learn . " 

 Deronda would not admit that he care about the risk , and he have really be beguile into a little indifference by double sympathy : he be very anxious that Hans should not miss the much - need scholarship , and he feel a revival of interest in the old study . still , when Hans , rather late in the day , get able to use his own eye , Deronda have tenacity enough to try hard and recover his lose ground . he fail , however ; but he have the satisfaction of see Meyrick win . 

 success , as a sort of beginning that urge completion , might have reconcile Deronda to his university course ; but the emptiness of all thing , from politic to pastime , be never so strike to we as when we fail in they . the loss of the personal triumph have no severity for he , but the sense of have spend his time ineffectively in a mode of working which have be against the grain , give he a distaste for any renewal of the process , which turn his imagined project of quit Cambridge into a serious intention . in speak of his intention to Meyrick he make it appear that he be glad of the turn event have take glad to have the balance dip decidedly , and feel freed from his hesitation ; but he observe that he must of course submit to any strong objection on the part of Sir Hugo . 

 Meyrick 's joy and gratitude be disturb by much uneasiness . he believe in Deronda 's alleged preference , but he feel keenly that in serve he Daniel have place himself at a disadvantage in Sir Hugo 's opinion , and he say mournfully , " if you have get the scholarship , Sir Hugo would have think that you ask to leave we with a well grace . you have spoil your luck for my sake , and I can do nothing to amend it . " 

 " yes , you can ; you be to be a first - rate fellow . I call that a first - rate investment of my luck . " 

 " oh , confound it ! you save an ugly mongrel from drown , and expect he to cut a fine figure . the poet have make tragedy enough about sign one 's self over to wickedness for the sake of get something plummy ; I shall write a tragedy of a fellow who sign himself over to be good , and be uncomfortable ever after . " 

 but Hans lose no time in secretly write the history of the affair to Sir Hugo , make it plain that but for Deronda 's generous devotion he could hardly have fail to win the prize he have be work for . 

 the two friend go up to town together : Meyrick to rejoice with his mother and the girl in their little home at Chelsea ; Deronda to carry out the less easy task of open his mind to Sir Hugo . he rely a little on the baronet 's general tolerance of eccentricity , but he expect more opposition than he meet with . he be receive with even warm kindness than usual , the failure be pass over lightly , and when he detail his reason for wish to quit the university and go to study abroad . Sir Hugo sit for some time in a silence which be rather meditative than surprised . at last he say , look at Daniel with examination , " so you do not   want to be an Englishman to the backbone after all ? " 

 " I want to be an Englishman , but I want to understand other point of view . and I want to get rid of a merely english attitude in study . " 

 " I see ; you do not   want to be turn out in the same mould as every other youngster . and I have nothing to say against your doffing some of our national prejudice . I feel the well myself for have spend a good deal of my time abroad . but , for God 's sake , keep an english cut , and do not   become indifferent to bad tobacco ! and , my dear boy , it be good to be unselfish and generous ; but do not   carry that too far . it will not do to give yourself to be melt down for the benefit of the tallow - trade ; you must know where to find yourself . however , I shall put no vote on your going . wait until I can get off Committee , and I will run over with you . " 

 so Deronda go accord to his will . but not before he have spend some hour with Hans Meyrick , and be introduce to the mother and sister in the Chelsea home . the shy girl watch and register every look of their brother 's friend , declare by Hans to have be the salvation of he , a fellow like nobody else , and , in fine , a brick . they so thoroughly accept Deronda as an ideal , that when he be go the young set to work , under the criticism of the two elder girl , to paint he as Prince Camaralzaman . 

 CHAPTER XVII . 

  " this be truth the poet sing , 
  that a sorrow 's crown of sorrow be remember happy thing . " 
  TENNYSON : Locksley Hall . 
 on a fine evening near the end of July , Deronda be row himself on the Thames . it be already a year or more since he have come back to England , with the understanding that his education be finish , and that he be somehow to take his place in english society ; but though , in deference to Sir Hugo 's wish , and to fence off idleness , he have begin to read law , this apparent decision have be without other result than to deepen the root of indecision . his old love of boat have revive with the more force now that he be in town with the Mallingers , because he could nowhere else get the same still seclusion which the river give he . he have a boat of his own at Putney , and whenever Sir Hugo do not want he , it be his chief holiday to row till past sunset and come in again with the star . not that he be in a sentimental stage ; but he be in another sort of contemplative mood perhaps more common in the young man of our day that of question whether it be worth while to take part in the battle of the world : I mean , of course , the young man in whom the unproductive labor of questioning be sustain by three or five per cent , on capital which somebody else have battle for . it puzzle Sir Hugo that one who make a splendid contrast with all that be sickly and pule should be hamper with idea which , since they leave an accomplished Whig like himself unobstructe , could be no well than spectral illusion ; especially as Deronda set himself against authorship a vocation which be understand to turn foolish thinking into fund . 

 row in his dark - blue shirt and skull - cap , his curl closely clip , his mouth beset with abundant soft wave of beard , he bear only disguised trace of the seraphic boy " trail cloud of glory . " still , even one who have never see he since his boyhood might have look at he with slow recognition , due perhaps to the peculiarity of the gaze which Gwendolen choose to call " dreadful , " though it have really a very mild sort of scrutiny . the voice , sometimes audible in subdued snatch of song , have turn out merely a high baritone ; indeed , only to look at his lithe , powerful frame and the firm gravity of his face would have be enough for an experienced guess that he have no rare and ravish tenor such as nature reluctantly make at some sacrifice . look at his hand : they be not small and dimpled , with taper finger that seem to have only a deprecating touch : they be long , flexible , firmly - grasp hand , such as Titian have paint in a picture where he want to show the combination of refinement with force . and there be something of a likeness , too , between the face belong to the hand in both the uniform pale - brown skin , the perpendicular brow , the calmly penetrate eye . not seraphic any long : thoroughly terrestrial and manly ; but still of a kind to raise belief in a human dignity which can afford to recognize poor relation . 

 such type meet we here and there among average condition ; in a workman , for example , whistle over a bit of measurement and lift his eye to answer our question about the road . and often the grand meaning of face as well as of write word may lie chiefly in the impression that happen just now to be of importance in relation to Deronda , row on the Thames in a very ordinary equipment for a young Englishman at leisure , and pass under Kew Bridge with no thought of an adventure in which his appearance be likely to play any part . in fact , he object very strongly to the notion , which other have not allow he to escape , that his appearance be of a kind to draw attention ; and hint of this , intend to be complimentary , find an angry resonance in he , come from mingled experience , to which a clue have already be give . his own face in the glass have during many year associate for he with thought of some one whom he must be like one about whose character and lot he continually wonder , and never dare to ask . 

 in the neighborhood of Kew Bridge , between six and seven o'clock , the river be no solitude . several person be saunter on the towing - path , and here and there a boat be ply . Deronda have be row fast to get over this spot , when , become aware of a great barge advance toward he , he guide his boat aside , and rest on his oar within a couple of yard of the river - brink . he be all the while unconsciously continue the low - toned chant which have haunt his throat all the way up the river the gondolier 's song in the " Otello , " where Rossini have worthily set to music the immortal word of Dante 

  " Nessun maggior dolore 
  Che ricordarsi del tempo felice 
  Nella miseria " : 
  Footnote : Dante 's word be well render by our own poet in the line 
 at the head of the chapter . 
 and , as he rest on his oar , the pianissimo fall of the melodic wail " nella miseria " be distinctly audible on the brink of the water . three or four person have pause at various spot to watch the barge pass the bridge , and doubtless include in their notice the young gentleman in the boat ; but probably it be only to one ear that the low vocal sound come with more significance than if they have be an insect - murmur amidst the sum of current noise . Deronda , await the barge , now turn his head to the river - side , and see at a few yard ' distant from he a figure which might have be an impersonation of the misery he be unconsciously give voice to : a girl hardly more than eighteen , of low slim figure , with most delicate little face , her dark curl push behind her ear under a large black hat , a long woolen cloak over her shoulder . her hand be hang down clasp before she , and her eye be fix on the river with a look of immovable , statue - like despair . this strong arrest of his attention make he cease singing : apparently his voice have enter her inner world without she take any note of whence it come , for when it suddenly cease she change her attitude slightly , and , look round with a frightened glance , meet Deronda 's face . it be but a couple of moment , but that seem a long while for two people to look straight at each other . her look be something like that of a fawn or other gentle animal before it turn to run away : no blush , no special alarm , but only some timidity which yet could not hinder she from a long look before she turn . in fact , it seem to Deronda that she be only half conscious of her surrounding : be she hungry , or be there some other cause of bewilderment ? he feel an outleap of interest and compassion toward she ; but the next instant she have turn and walk away to a neighboring bench under a tree . he have no right to linger and watch she : poorly - dress , melancholy woman be common sight ; it be only the delicate beauty , picturesque line and color of the image that be exceptional , and these condition make it more markedly impossible that he should obtrude his interest upon she . he begin to row away and be soon far up the river ; but no other thought be busy enough quite to expel that pale image of unhappy girlhood . he fall again and again to speculate on the probable romance that lie behind that loneliness and look of desolation ; then to smile at his own share in the prejudice that interesting face must have interesting adventure ; then to justify himself for feel that sorrow be the more tragic when it befell delicate , childlike beauty . 

 " I should not have forget the look of misery if she have be ugly and vulgar , " he say to himself . but there be no deny that the attractiveness of the image make it likeli to last . it be clear to he as an onyx cameo ; the brown - black drapery , the white face with small , small feature and dark , long - lash eye . his mind glance over the girl - tragedy that be go on in the world , hide , unheeded , as if they be but tragedy of the copse or hedgerow , where the helpless drag wound wing forsakenly , and streak the shadowed moss with the red moment - hand of their own death . Deronda of late , in his solitary excursion , have be occupy chiefly with uncertainty about his own course ; but those uncertainty , be much at their leisure , be will not to have such wide - sweep connection with all life and history that the new image of helpless sorrow easily blent itself with what seem to he the strong array of reason why he should shrink from get into that routine of the world which make man apologize for all its wrong - doing , and take opinion as mere professional equipment why he should not draw strongly at any thread in the hopelessly - entangled scheme of thing . 

 he use his oar little , satisfied to go with the tide and be take back by it . it be his habit to indulge himself in that solemn passivity which easily come with the lengthen shadow and mellow light , when think and desiring melt together imperceptibly , and what in other hour may have seem argument take the quality of passionate vision . by the time he have come back again with the tide past Richmond Bridge the sun be near set : and the approach of his favorite hour with its deepening stillness and darken mass of tree and building between the double glow of the sky and the river dispose he to linger as if they have be an unfinished strain of music . he look out for a perfectly solitary spot where he could lodge his boat against the bank , and , throw himself on his back with his head prop on the cushion , could watch out the light of sunset and the opening of that bead - roll which some oriental poet describe as God 's call to the little star , who each answer , " here be I. " he choose a spot in the bend of the river just opposite Kew Gardens , where he have a great breadth of water before he reflect the glory of the sky , while he himself be in shadow . he lie with his hand behind his head , prop on a level with the boat 's edge , so that he could see all round he , but could not be see by any one at a few yard ' distance ; and for a long while he never turn his eye from the view right in front of he . he be forget everything else in a half - speculative , half - involuntary identification of himself with the object he be look at , think how far it might be possible habitually to shift his centre till his own personality would be no less outside he than the landscape when the sense of something move on the bank opposite he where it be border by a line of willow bush , make he turn his glance thitherward . in the first moment he have a dart presentiment about the move figure ; and now he could see the small face with the strange die sunlight upon it . he fear to frighten she by a sudden movement , and watch she with motionless attention . she look round , but seem only to gather security from the apparent solitude , hide her hat among the willow , and immediately take off her woolen cloak . presently she seat herself and deliberately dip the cloak in the water , hold it there a little while , then take it out with effort , rise from her seat as she do so . by this time Deronda feel sure that she mean to wrap the wet cloak round she as a drown shroud ; there be no long time to hesitate about frighten she . he rise and seize his oar to ply across ; happily her position lie a little below he . the poor thing , overcome with terror at this sign of discovery from the opposite bank , sink down on the brink again , hold her cloak half out of the water . she crouch and cover her face as if she keep a faint hope that she have not be see , and that the boatman be accidentally come toward she . but soon he be within brief space of she , steady his boat against the bank , and speak , but very gently 

 " do not   be afraid . you be unhappy . pray , trust I . tell I what I can do to help you . " 

 she raise her head and look up at he . his face now be toward the light , and she know it again . but she do not speak for a few moment which be a renewal of their former gaze at each other . at last she say in a low sweet voice , with an accent so distinct that it suggest foreignness and yet be not foreign , " I see you before , " and then add dreamily , after a like pause , " nella miseria . " 

 Deronda , not understand the connection of her thought , suppose that her mind be weaken by distress and hunger . 

 " it be you , sing ? " she go on , hesitatingly " Nessun maggior dolore . " the mere word themselves utter in her sweet undertone seem to give the melody to Deronda 's ear . 

 " ah , yes , " he say , understand now , " I be often sing they . but I fear you will injure yourself stay here . pray let I take you in my boat to some place of safety . and that wet cloak let I take it . " 

 he would not attempt to take it without her leave , dread lest he should scare she . even at his word , he fancy that she shrink and clutch the cloak more tenaciously . but her eye be fix on he with a question in they as she say , " you look good . perhaps it be God 's command . " 

 " do trust I . let I help you . I will die before I will let any harm come to you . " 

 she rise from her sit posture , first drag the saturate cloak and then let it fall on the ground it be too heavy for her tired arm . her little woman 's figure as she lay her delicate chill hand together one over the other against her waist , and go a step backward while she lean her head forward as if not to lose sight of his face , be unspeakably touching . 

 " Great God ! " the word escape Deronda in a tone so low and solemn that they seem like a prayer become unconsciously vocal . the agitate impression this forsake girl be make on he stir a fibre that lie close to his deep interest in the fate of woman " perhaps my mother be like this one . " the old thought have come now with a new impetus of mingle feeling , and urge that exclamation in which both East and West have for age concentrate their awe in the presence of inexorable calamity . 

 the low - tone word seem to have some reassurance in they for the hearer : she step forward close to the boat 's side , and Deronda put out his hand , hop now that she would let he help she in . she have already put her tiny hand into his which close around it , when some new thought strike she , and draw back she say 

 " I have nowhere to go nobody belong to I in all this land . " 

 " I will take you to a lady who have daughter , " say Deronda , immediately . he feel a sort of relief in gather that the wretched home and cruel friend he imagine she to be flee from be not in the near background . still she hesitate , and say more timidly than ever 

 " do you belong to the theatre ? " 

 " no ; I have nothing to do with the theatre , " say Deronda , in a decided tone . then beseechingly , " I will put you in perfect safety at once ; with a lady , a good woman ; I be sure she will be kind . let we lose no time : you will make yourself ill . life may still become sweet to you . there be good people there be good woman who will take care of you . " 

 she draw backward no more , but step in easily , as if she be use to such action , and sit down on the cushion . 

 " you have a covering for your head , " say Deronda . 

 " my hat ? "   she lift up her hand to her head .   " it be quite hide in the bush . " 

 " I will find it , " say Deronda , put out his hand deprecatingly as she attempt to rise . " the boat be fix . " 

 he jump out , find the hat , and lift up the saturate cloak , wring it and throw it into the bottom of the boat . 

 " we must carry the cloak away , to prevent any one who may have notice you from think you have be drown , " he say , cheerfully , as he get in again and present the old hat to she . " I wish I have any other garment than my coat to offer you . but shall you mind throw it over your shoulder while we be on the water ? it be quite an ordinary thing to do , when people return late and be not enough provide with wrap . " he hold out the coat toward she with a smile , and there come a faint melancholy smile in answer , as she take it and put it on very cleverly . 

 " I have some biscuit should you like they ? " say Deronda . 

 " no ; I can not eat . I have still some money leave to buy bread . " 

 he begin to ply his oar without further remark , and they go along swiftly for many minute without speak . she do not look at he , but be watch the oar , lean forward in an attitude of repose , as if she be begin to feel the comfort of return warmth and the prospect of life instead of death . the twilight be deepen ; the red flush be all go and the little star be give their answer one after another . the moon be rise , but be still entangle among the tree and building . the light be not such that he could distinctly discern the expression of her feature or her glance , but they be distinctly before he nevertheless feature and a glance which seem to have give a fuller meaning for he to the human face . among his anxiety one be dominant : his first impression about she , that her mind might be disorder , have not be quite dissipate : the project of suicide be unmistakable , and give a deep color to every other suspicious sign . he long to begin a conversation , but abstain , wish to encourage the confidence that might induce she to speak first . at last she do speak . 

 " I like to listen to the oar . " 

 " so do I. " 

 " if you have not come , I should have be dead now . " 

 " I can not bear you to speak of that . I hope you will never be sorry that I come . " 

 " I can not see how I shall be glad to live . the maggior dolore and the miseria have last long than the tempo felice . " she pause and then go on dreamily , " dolore miseria I think those word be alive . " 

 Deronda be mute : to question she seem an unwarrantable freedom ; he shrink from appear to claim the authority of a benefactor , or to treat she with the less reverence because she be in distress . she go on musingly 

 " I think it be not wicked . death and life be one before the Eternal . I know our father slew their child and then slay themselves , to keep their soul pure . I mean it so . but now I be command to live . I can not see how I shall live . " 

 " you will find friend . I will find they for you . " 

 she shake her head and say mournfully , " not my mother and brother . I can not find they . " 

 " you be english ? you must be speak English so perfectly . " 

 she do not answer immediately , but look at Deronda again , strain to see he in the double light . until now she have be watch the oar . it seem as if she be half rouse , and wonder which part of her impression be dream and which wake . sorrowful isolation have benumb her sense of reality , and the power of distinguish outward and inward be continually slip away from she . her look be full of wonder timidity such as the forsaken one in the desert might have lift to the angelic vision before she know whether his message be in anger or in pity . 

 " you want to know if I be english ? " she say at last , while Deronda be redden nervously under a gaze which he feel more fully than he see . 

 " I want to know nothing except what you like to tell I , " he say , still uneasy in the fear that her mind be wander . " perhaps it be not good for you to talk . " 

 " yes , I will tell you . I be English - bear . but I be a Jewess . " 

 Deronda be silent , inwardly wonder that he have not say this to himself before , though any one who have see delicate - face spanish girl might simply have guess she to be spanish . 

 " do you despise I for it ? " she say presently in low tone , which have a sadness that pierce like a cry from a small dumb creature in fear . 

 " why should I ? " say Deronda . " I be not so foolish . " 

 " I know many Jews be bad . " 

 " so be many Christians . but I should not think it fair for you to despise I because of that . " 

 " my mother and brother be good . but I shall never find they . I be come a long way from abroad . I run away ; but I can not tell you I can not speak of it . I think I might find my mother again God would guide I . but then I despair . this morning when the light come , I feel as if one word keep sound within I never ! never ! but now I begin to think " her word be break by rise sob " I be command to live perhaps we be go to she . " 

 with an outburst of weep she bury her head on her knee . he hop that this passionate weeping might relieve her excitement . meanwhile he be inwardly picture in much embarrassment how he should present himself with she in Park Lane the course which he have at first unreflectingly determined on . no one kind and more gentle than Lady Mallinger ; but it be hardly probable that she would be at home ; and he have a shudder sense of a lackey star at this delicate , sorrowful image of womanhood of glare light and fine staircase , and perhaps chill suspicious manner from lady 's maid and housekeeper , that might scare the mind already in a state of dangerous susceptibility . but to take she to any other shelter than a home already know to he be not to be contemplate : he be full of fear about the issue of the adventure which have bring on he a responsibility all the heavy for the strong and agitate impression this childlike creature have make on he . but another resource come to mind : he could venture to take she to Mrs. Meyrick 's to the small house at Chelsea where he have be often enough since his return from abroad to feel sure that he could appeal there to generous heart , which have a romantic readiness to believe in innocent need and to help it . Hans Meyrick be safe away in Italy , and Deronda feel the comfort of present himself with his charge at a house where he would be meet by a motherly figure of quakerish neatness , and three girl who hardly know of any evil close to they than what lie in history - book , and drama , and would at once associate a lovely Jewess with Rebecca in " Ivanhoe , " besides think that everything they do at Deronda 's request would be do for their idol , Hans . the vision of the Chelsea home once raise , Deronda no long hesitate . 

 the rumble thither in the cab after the stillness of the water seem long . happily his charge have be quiet since her fit of weeping , and submit like a tired child . when they be in the cab , she lay down her hat and try to rest her head , but the jolting movement would not let it rest . still she doze , and her sweet head hang helpless , first on one side , then on the other . 

 " they be too good to have any fear about take she in , " think Deronda . her person , her voice , her exquisite utterance , be one strong appeal to belief and tenderness . yet what have be the history which have bring she to this desolation ? he be go on a strange errand to ask shelter for this waif . then there occur to he the beautiful story Plutarch somewhere tell of the delphic woman : how when the maenad , outwear with their torch - light wandering , lie down to sleep in the market - place , the matron come and stand silently round they to keep guard over their slumber ; then , when they wake , minister to they tenderly and see they safely to their own border . he could trust the woman he be go to for have heart as good . 

 Deronda feel himself grow old this evening and enter on a new phase in find a life to which his own have come perhaps as a rescue ; but how to make sure that snatch from death be rescue ? the moment of find a fellow - creature be often as full of mingled doubt and exultation as the moment of find an idea . 

 CHAPTER XVIII . 

  Life be a various mother : now she don 
  her plume and brilliant , climb the marble stair 
  with head aloft , nor ever turn her eye 
  on lackey who attend she ; now she dwell 
  grim - clad , up darksome allyes , breathe hot gin , 
  and scream in pauper riot . 
  but to these 
  she come a frugal matron , neat and deft , 
  with cheerful morning thought and quick device 
  to find the much in little . 
 Mrs. Meyrick 's house be not noisy : the front parlor look on the river , and the back on garden , so that though she be read aloud to her daughter , the window could be leave open to freshen the air of the small double room where a lamp and two candle be burn . the candle be on a table apart for Kate , who be draw illustration for a publisher ; the lamp be not only for the reader but for Amy and Mab , who be embroider satin cushion for " the great world . " 

 outside , the house look very narrow and shabby , the bright light through the holland blind show the heavy old - fashioned window - frame ; but it be pleasant to know that many such grim - walled slice of space in our foggy London have be and still be the home of a culture the more spotlessly free from vulgarity , because poverty have render everything like display an impersonal question , and all the grand show of the world simply a spectacle which rouse petty rivalry or vain effort after possession . 

 the Meyricks ' be a home of that kind : and they all cling to this particular house in a row because its interior be fill with object always in the same place , which , for the mother hold memory of her marriage time , and for the young one seem as necessary and uncriticise a part of their world as the star of the Great Bear see from the back window . Mrs. Meyrick have bear much stint of other matter that she might be able to keep some engraving specially cherish by her husband ; and the narrow space of wall hold a world history in scene and head which the child have early learn by heart . the chair and table be also old friend prefer to new . but in these two little parlor with no furniture that a broker would have care to cheapen except the print and piano , there be space and apparatus for a wide - glance , nicely - select life , open to the high thing in music , painting and poetry . I be not sure that in the time of great scarcity , before Kate could get pay - work , these lady have always have a servant to light their fire and sweep their room ; yet they be fastidious in some point , and could not believe that the manner of lady in the fashionable world be so full of coarse selfishness , petty quarreling , and slang as they be represent to be in what be call literary photograph . the Meyricks have their little oddity , streak of eccentricity from the mother 's blood as well as the father 's , their mind be like mediaval house with unexpected recess and opening from this into that , flight of step and sudden outlook . 

 but mother and daughter be all unite by a triple bond family love ; admiration for the fine work , the good action ; and habitual industry . Hans ' desire to spend some of his money in make their life more luxurious have be resist by all of they , and both they and he have be thus save from regret at the threaten triumph of his yearning for art over the attraction of secured income a triumph that would by - and - by oblige he to give up his fellowship . they could all afford to laugh at his Gavarni - caricature and to hold he blameless in follow a natural bent which their unselfishness and independence have leave without obstacle . it be enough for they to go on in their old way , only have a grand treat of opera - go   to the gallery   when Hans come home on a visit . 

 see the group they make this evening , one could hardly wish they to change their way of life . they be all alike small , and so in due proportion to their miniature room . Mrs. Meyrick be read aloud from a french book ; she be a lively little woman , half french , half Scotch , with a pretty articulateness of speech that seem to make daylight in her hearer 's understanding . though she be not yet fifty , her ripple hair , cover by a quakerish net cap , be chiefly gray , but her eyebrow be brown as the bright eye below they ; her black dress , almost like a priest 's cassock with its row of button , suit a neat figure hardly five foot high . the daughter be to match the mother , except that Mab have Hans ' light hair and complexion , with a bossy , irregular brow , and other quaintness that remind one of he . everything about they be compact , from the firm coil of their hair , fasten back a la Chinoise , to their gray skirt in Puritan nonconformity with the fashion , which at that time would have demand that four feminine circumference should fill all the free space in the front parlor . all four , if they have be wax - work , might have be pack easily in a fashionable lady 's travel trunk . their face seem full of speech , as if their mind have be shell , after the manner of horse - chestnut , and become brightly visible . the only large thing of its kind in the room be Hafiz , the persian cat , comfortably poise on the brown leather back of a chair , and open his large eye now and then to see that the low animal be not in any mischief . 

 the book Mrs. Meyrick have before she be Erckmann - Chatrian 's Historie d'un Conscrit . she have just finish read it aloud , and Mab , who have let her work fall on the ground while she stretch her head forward and fix her eye on the reader , exclaim 

 " I think that be the fine story in the world . " 

 " of course , Mab ! " say Amy , " it be the last you have hear . everything that please you be the good in its turn . " 

 " it be hardly to be call a story , " say Kate . " it be a bit of history bring near we with a strong telescope . we can see the soldier ' face : no , it be more than that we can hear everything we can almost hear their heart beat . " 

 " I do not   care what you call it , " say Mab , flirt away her thimble . " call it a chapter in Revelations . it make I want to do something good , something grand . it make I so sorry for everybody . it make I like Schiller I want to take the world in my arm and kiss it . I must kiss you instead , little mother ? " she throw her arm round her mother 's neck . 

 " whenever you be in that mood , Mab , down go your work , " say Amy . " it would be do something good to finish your cushion without soil it . " 

 " oh oh oh ! " groan Mab , as she stoop to pick up her work and thimble . " I wish I have three wound conscript to take care of . " 

 " you would spill their beef - tea while you be talk , " say Amy . 

 " Poor Mab ! do not   be hard on she , " say the mother . " give I the embroidery now , child . you go on with your enthusiasm , and I will go on with the pink and white poppy . " 

 " well , ma , I think you be more caustic than Amy , " say Kate , while she draw her head back to look at her drawing . 

 " oh oh oh ! " cry Mab again , rise and stretch her arm . " I wish something wonderful would happen . I feel like the deluge . the water of the great deep be break up , and the window of heaven be open . I must sit down and play the scale . " 

 Mab be open the piano while the other be laugh at this climax , when a cab stop before the house , and there forthwith come a quick rap of the knocker . 

 " dear I ! " say Mrs. Meyrick , start up , " it be after ten , and Phoebe be go to bed . " she hasten out , leave the parlor door open . 

 " Mr. Deronda ! " the girl could hear this exclamation from their mamma . Mab clasp her hand , say in a loud whisper , " there now ! something be go to happen . " Kate and Amy give up their work in amazement . but Deronda 's tone in reply be so low that they could not hear his word , and Mrs. Meyrick immediately close the parlor door . 

 " I know I be trust to your goodness in a most extraordinary way , " Deronda go on , after give his brief narrative ; " but you can imagine how helpless I feel with a young creature like this on my hand . I could not go with she among stranger , and in her nervous state I should dread take she into a house full of servant . I have trust to your mercy . I hope you will not think my act unwarrantable . " 

 " on the contrary . you have honor I by trust I . I see your difficulty . pray bring she in . I will go and prepare the girl . " 

 while Deronda go back to the cab , Mrs. Meyrick turn into the parlor again and say : " here be somebody to take care of instead of your wound conscript , Mab : a poor girl who be go to drown herself in despair . Mr. Deronda find she only just in time to save she . he bring she along in his boat , and do not know what else it would be safe to do with she , so he have trust we and bring she here . it seem she be a Jewess , but quite refine , he say know italian and music . " 

 the three girl , wonder and expectant , come forward and stand near each other in mute confidence that they be all feel alike under this appeal to their compassion . Mab look rather awe - stricken , as if this answer to her wish be something preternatural . 

 meanwhile Deronda go to the door of the cab where the pale face be now gaze out with roused observation , say , " I have bring you to some of the kind people in the world : there be daughter like you . it be a happy home . will you let I take you to they ? " 

 she step out obediently , put her hand in his and forget her hat ; and when Deronda lead she into the full light of the parlor where the four little woman stand await she , she make a picture that would have stir much dull sensibility than theirs . at first she be a little daze by the sudden light , and before she have concentrate her glance he have put her hand into the mother 's . he be inwardly rejoice that the Meyricks be so small : the dark - curl head be the high among they . the poor wanderer could not be afraid of these gentle face so near her : and now she be look at each of they in turn while the mother say , " you must be weary , poor child . " 

 " we will take care of you we will comfort you we will love you , " cry Mab , no long able to restrain herself , and take the small right hand caressingly between both her own . this gentle welcome warmth be penetrate the bewildered one : she hang back just enough to see well the four face in front of she , whose good will be be reflect in her , not in any smile , but in that undefinable change which tell we that anxiety be pass in contentment . for an instant she look up at Deronda , as if she be refer all this mercy to he , and then again turn to Mrs. Meyrick , say with more collectedness in her sweet tone than he have hear before 

 " I be a stranger . I be a Jewess . you might have think I be wicked . " 

 " no , we be sure you be good , " burst out Mab . 

 " we think no evil of you , poor child . you shall be safe with we , " say Mrs. Meyrick . " come now and sit down . you must have some food , and then you must go to rest . " 

 the stranger look up again at Deronda , who say 

 " you will have no more fear with these friend ? you will rest to - night ? " 

 " oh , I should not fear . I should rest . I think these be the minister angel . " 

 Mrs. Meyrick want to lead she to seat , but again hang back gently , the poor weary thing speak as if with a scruple at be receive without a further account of herself . 

 " my name be Mirah Lapidoth . I be come a long way , all the way from Prague by myself . I make my escape . I run away from dreadful thing . I come to find my mother and brother in London . I have be take from my mother when I be little , but I think I could find she again . I have trouble the house be all go I could not find she . it have be a long while , and I have not much money . that be why I be in distress . " 

 " our mother will be good to you , " cry Mab . " see what a nice little mother she be ! " 

 " do sit down now , " say Kate , move a chair forward , while Amy run to get some tea . 

 Mirah resist no long , but seat herself with perfect grace , cross her little foot , lay her hand one over the other on her lap , and look at her friend with placid reverence ; whereupon Hafiz , who have be watch the scene restlessly come forward with tail erect and rub himself against her ankle . Deronda feel it time to go . 

 " will you allow I to come again and inquire perhaps at five to - morrow ? " he say to Mrs. Meyrick . 

 " yes , pray ; we shall have have time to make acquaintance then . " 

 " good - bye , " say Deronda , look down at Mirah , and put out his hand . she rise as she take it , and the moment bring back to they both strongly the other moment when she have first take that outstretche hand . she lift her eye to his and say with reverential fervor , " the God of our father bless you and deliver you from all evil as you have deliver I . I do not believe there be any man so good . none before have think I worthy of the good . you find I poor and miserable , yet you have give I the good . " 

 Deronda could not speak , but with silent adieux to the Meyricks , hurry away . 

 BOOK III maidens choosing . 

 CHAPTER XIX . 

 " I pity the man who can travel from Dan to Beersheba , and say , ' tis all barren ' : and so it be : and so be all the world to he who will not cultivate the fruit it offer . " STERNE : Sentimental Journey . 

 to say that Deronda be romantic would be to misrepresent he ; but under his calm and somewhat self - repress exterior there be a fervor which make he easily find poetry and romance among the event of every - day life . and perhaps poetry and romance be as plentiful as ever in the world except for those phlegmatic nature who I suspect would in any age have regard they as a dull form of erroneous thinking . they exist very easily in the same room with the microscope and even in railway carriage : what banish they in the vacuum in gentleman and lady passenger . how should all the apparatus of heaven and earth , from the farth firmament to the tender bosom of the mother who nourish we , make poetry for a mind that have no movement of awe and tenderness , no sense of fellowship which thrill from the near to the distant , and back again from the distant to the near ? 

 to Deronda this event of find Mirah be as heart - stir as anything that befell Orestes or Rinaldo . he sit up half the night , live again through the moment since he have first discern Mirah on the river - brink , with the fresh and fresh vividness which belong to emotive memory . when he take up a book to try and dull this urgency of inward vision , the print word be no more than a network through which he see and hear everything as clearly as before see not only the actual event of two hour , but possibility of what have be and what might be which those event be enough to feed with the warm blood of passionate hope and fear . something in his own experience cause Mirah 's search after her mother to lay hold with peculiar force on his imagination . the first prompting of sympathy be to aid she in her search : if give person be extant in London there be way of find they , as subtle as scientific experiment , the right machinery be set at work . but here the mixed feeling which belong to Deronda 's kindre experience naturally transfuse themselves into his anxiety on behalf of Mirah . 

 the desire to know his own mother , or to know about she , be constantly haunt with dread ; and in imagine what might befall Mirah it quickly occur to he that find the mother and brother from whom she have be part when she be a little one might turn out to be a calamity . when she be in the boat she say that her mother and brother be good ; but the goodness might have be chiefly in her own ignorant innocence and yearn memory , and the ten or twelve year since the parting have be time enough for much worsening . spite of his strong tendency to side with the object of prejudice , and in general with those who get the bad of it , his interest have never be practically draw toward exist Jews , and the fact he know about they , whether they walk conspicuous in fine apparel or lurk in by - street , be chiefly of a sort most repugnant to he . of learn and accomplish Jews he take it for grant that they have drop their religion , and wish to be merge in the people of their native land . Scorn fling at a Jew as such would have rouse all his sympathy in grief of inheritance ; but the indiscriminate scorn of a race will often strike a speciman who have well earn it on his own account , and might fairly be gibbet as a rascally son of Adam . it appear that the Caribs , who know little of theology , regard thieving as a practice peculiarly connect with christian tenet , and probably they could allege experimental ground for this opinion . Deronda could not escape   who can ?   know ugly story of jewish characteristic and occupation ; and though one of his favorite protest be against the severance of past and present history , he be like other who share his protest , in never have care to reach any more special conclusion about actual Jews than that they retain the virtue and vice of a long - oppress race . but now that Mirah 's longing rouse his mind to a close survey of detail , very disagreeable image urge themselves of what it might be to find out this middle - aged Jewess and her son . to be sure , there be the exquisite refinement and charm of the creature herself to make a presumption in favor of her immediate kindred , but he must wait to know more : perhaps through Mrs. Meyrick he might gather some guide hint from Mirah 's own lip . her voice , her accent , her look all the sweet purity that clothe she as with a consecrating garment make he shrink the more from give she , either ideally or practically , an association with what be hateful or contaminate . but these fine word with which we fumigate and becloud unpleasant fact be not the language in which we think . Deronda 's thinking go on in rapid image of what might be : he see himself guide by some official scout into a dingy street ; he enter through a dim doorway , and see a hawk - eyed woman , rough - headed , and unwashed , cheapen a hungry girl 's last bit of finery ; or in some quarter only the more hideous for be smart , he find himself under the breath of a young Jew talkative and familiar , willing to show his acquaintance with gentleman 's taste , and not fastidious in any transaction with which they would favor he and so on through the brief chapter of his experience in this kind . excuse he : his mind be not apt to run spontaneously into insult idea , or to practice a form of wit which identify Moses with the advertisement sheet ; but he be just now govern by dread , and if Mirah 's parent have be Christian , the chief difference would have be that his foreboding would have be feed with wide knowledge . it be the habit of his mind to connect dread with unknown parentage , and in this case as well as his own there be enough to make the connection reasonable . 

 but what be to be do with Mirah ? she need shelter and protection in the full sense , and all his chivalrous sentiment rouse itself to insist that the soon and the more fully he could engage for she the interest of other besides himself , the well he should fulfill her claim on he . he have no right to provide for she entirely , though he might be able to do so ; the very depth of the impression she have produce make he desire that she should understand herself to be entirely independent of he ; and vague vision of the future which he try to dispel as fantastic leave their influence in an anxiety strong than any motive he could give for it , that those who see his action closely should be acquaint from the first with the history of his relation to Mirah . he have learn to hate secrecy about the grand tie and obligation of his life to hate it the more because a strong spell of interwoven sensibility hinder he from break such secrecy . Deronda have make a vow to himself that since the truth which disgrace mortal be not all of their own make the truth should never be make a disgrace to another by his act . he be not without terror lest he should break this vow , and fall into the apologetic philosophy which explain the world into contain nothing well than one 's own conduct . 

 at one moment he resolve to tell the whole of his adventure to Sir Hugo and Lady Mallinger the next morning at breakfast , but the possibility that something quite new might reveal itself on his next visit to Mrs. Meyrick 's check this impulse , and he finally go to sleep on the conclusion that he would wait until that visit have be make . 

 CHAPTER XX . 

 " it will hardly be deny that even in this frail and corrupted world , we sometimes meet person who , in their very mien and aspect , as well as in the whole habit of life , manifest such a signature and stamp of virtue , as to make our judgment of they a matter of intuition rather than the result of continued examination . " ALEXANDER KNOX : quote in Southey 's Life of Wesley . 

 Mirah say that she have sleep well that night ; and when she come down in Mab 's black dress , her dark hair curl in fresh fibril as it gradually dry from its plenteous bath , she look like one who be begin to take comfort after the long sorrow and watch which have pal her cheek and make blue semicircle under her eye . it be Mab who carry her breakfast and usher she down with some pride in the effect produce by a pair of tiny feel slipper which she have rush out to buy because there be no shoe in the house small enough for Mirah , whose borrow dress cease about her ankle and display the cheap clothing that , mould itself on her foot , seem an adornment as choice as the sheath of bud . the farthe buckle be bijoux . 

 " oh , if you please , mamma ? " cry Mab , clasp her hand and stoop toward Mirah 's foot , as she enter the parlor ; " look at the slipper , how beautiful they fit ! I declare she be like the Queen Budoor ' two delicate foot , the work of the protecting and all - recompense creator , support she ; and I wonder how they can sustain what be above they . ' " 

 Mirah look down at her own foot in a childlike way and then smile at Mrs. Meyrick , who be say inwardly , " one could hardly imagine this creature have an evil thought . but wise people would tell I to be cautious . " she return Mirah 's smile and say , " I fear the foot have have to sustain their burden a little too often lately . but to - day she will rest and be my companion . " 

 " and she will tell you so many thing and I shall not hear they , " grumble Mab , who feel herself in the first volume of a delightful romance and oblige to miss some chapter because she have to go to pupil . 

 Kate be already go to make sketch along the river , and Amy be away on business errand . it be what the mother wish , to be alone with this stranger , whose story must be a sorrowful one , yet be needful to be tell . 

 the small front parlor be as good as a temple that morning . the sunlight be on the river and soft air come in through the open window ; the wall show a glorious silent cloud of witness the Virgin soar amid her cherubic escort ; grand Melancholia with her solemn universe ; the prophet and Sibyls ; the School of Athens ; the last supper ; mystic group where far - off age make one moment ; grave Holbein and Rembrandt head ; the Tragic Muse ; last - century child at their musing or their play ; italian poet all be there through the medium of a little black and white . the neat mother who have weather her trouble , and come out of they with a face still cheerful , be sort colored wool for her embroidery . Hafiz purr on the window - ledge , the clock on the mantle - piece tick without hurry , and the occasional sound of wheel seem to lie outside the more massive central quiet . Mrs. Meyrick think that this quiet might be the good invitation to speech on the part of her companion , and choose not to disturb it by remark . Mirah sit opposite in her former attitude , her hand clasp on her lap , her ankle cross , her eye at first travel slowly over the object around she , but finally rest with a sort of placid reverence on Mrs. Meyrick . at length she begin to speak softly . 

 " I remember my mother 's face well than anything ; yet I be not seven when I be take away , and I be nineteen now . " 

 " I can understand that , " say Mrs. Meyrick . " there be some early thing that last the long . " 

 " oh , yes , it be the early . I think my life begin with wake up and love my mother 's face : it be so near to I , and her arm be round I , and she sing to I . one hymn she sing so often , so often : and then she teach I to sing it with she : it be the first I ever sing . they be always hebrew hymn she sing ; and because I never know the meaning of the word they seem full of nothing but our love and happiness . when I lay in my little bed and it be all white above I , she use to bend over I , between I and the white , and sing in a sweet , low voice . I can dream myself back into that time when I be awake , and it often come back to I in my sleep my hand be very little , I put it up to her face and she kiss it . sometimes in my dream I begin to tremble and think that we be both dead ; but then I wake up and my hand lie like this , and for a moment I hardly know myself . but if I could see my mother again I should know she . " 

 " you must expect some change after twelve year , " say Mrs. Meyrick , gently . " see my grey hair : ten year ago it be bright brown . the day and month pace over we like restless little bird , and leave the mark of their foot backward and forward ; especially when they be like bird with heavy heart - then they tread heavily . " 

 " ah , I be sure her heart have be heavy for want of I . but to feel her joy if we could meet again , and I could make she know I love she and give her deep comfort after all her mourning ! if that could be , I should mind nothing ; I should be glad that I have live through my trouble . I do despair . the world seem miserable and wicked ; none help I so that I could bear their look and word ; I feel that my mother be dead , and death be the only way to she . but then in the last moment yesterday , when I long for the water to close over I and I think that death be the good image of mercy then goodness come to I live , and I feel trust in the living . and it be strange but I begin to hope that she be live too . and now I with you here this morning , peace and hope have come into I like a flood . I want nothing ; I can wait ; because I hope and believe and be grateful oh , so grateful ! you have not think evil of I you have not despise I . " 

 Mirah speak with low - toned fervor , and sit as still as a picture all the while . 

 " many other would have feel as we do , my dear , " say Mrs. Meyrick , feel a mist come over her eye as she look at her work . 

 " but I do not meet they they do not come to I . " 

 " how be it that you be take from your mother ? " 

 " ah , I be a long while come to that . it be dreadful to speak of , yet I must tell you I must tell you everything . my father it be he that take I away . I think we be only go on a little journey ; and I be pleased . there be a box with all my little thing in . but we go on board a ship , and get far and far away from the land . then I be ill ; and I think it would never end it be the first misery , and it seem endless . but at last we land . I know nothing then , and believe what my father say . he comfort I , and tell I I should go back to my mother . but it be America we have reach , and it be long year before we come back to Europe . at first I often ask my father when we be go back ; and I try to learn write fast , because I want to write to my mother ; but one day when he find I try to write a letter , he take I on his knee and tell I that my mother and brother be dead ; that be why we do not go back . I remember my brother a little ; he carry I once ; but he be not always at home . I believe my father when he say that they be dead . I see they under the earth when he say they be there , with their eye forever close . I never think of its not be true ; and I use to cry every night in my bed for a long while . then when she come so often to I , in my sleep , I think she must be live about I though I could not always see she , and that comfort I . I be never afraid in the dark , because of that ; and very often in the day I use to shut my eye and bury my face and try to see she and to hear her singing . I come to do that at last without shut my eye . " 

 Mirah pause with a sweet content in her face , as if she be have her happy vision , while she look out toward the river . 

 " still your father be not unkind to you , I hope , " say Mrs. Meyrick , after a minute , anxious to recall she . 

 " no ; he pet I , and take pain to teach I . he be an actor ; and I find out , after , that the ' Coburg ' I use to hear of his go to at home be a theatre . but he have more to do with the theatre than act . he have not always be an actor ; he have be a teacher , and know many language . his acting be not very good ; I think , but he manage the stage , and write and translate play . an italian lady , a singer , live with we a long time . they both teach I , and I have a master besides , who make I learn by heart and recite . I work quite hard , though I be so little ; and I be not nine when I first go on the stage . I could easily learn thing , and I be not afraid . but then and ever since I hat our way of life . my father have money , and we have finery about we in a disorderly way ; always there be man and woman come and go ; there be loud laugh and disputing , strut , snap of finger , jeering , face I do not like to look at though many pet and caress I . but then I remember my mother . even at first when I understand nothing , I shrink away from all those thing outside I into companionship with thought that be not like they ; and I gather thought very fast , because I read many thing play and poetry , Shakespeare and Schiller , and learn evil and good . my father begin to believe that I might be a great singer : my voice be consider wonderful for a child ; and he have the good teaching for I . but it be painful that he boast of I , and set I to sing for show at any minute , as if I have be a musical box . once when I be nine year old , I play the part of a little girl who have be forsake and do not know it , and sit singing to herself while she play with flower . I do it without any trouble ; but the clapping and all the sound of the theatre be hateful to I ; and I never like the praise I have , because it all seem very hard and unloving : I miss the love and trust I have be bear into . I make a life in my own thought quite different from everything about I : I choose what seem to I beautiful out of the play and everything , and make my world out of it ; and it be like a sharp knife always graze I that we have two sort of life which jar so with each other woman look good and gentle on the stage , and say good thing as if they feel they , and directly after I see they with coarse , ugly manner . my father sometimes notice my shrink way ; and Signora say one day , when I have be rehearse , ' she will never be an artist : she have no notion of be anybody but herself . that do very well now , but by - and - by you will see she will have no more face and action than a singing - bird . ' my father be angry , and they quarrel . I sit alone and cry , because what she have say be like a long unhappy future unroll before I . I do not want to be an artist ; but this be what my father expect of I . after a while Signora leave we , and a governess use to come and give I lesson in different thing , because my father begin to be afraid of my singing too much ; but I still act from time to time . rebellious feeling grow strong in I , and I wish to get away from this life ; but I could not tell where to go , and I dread the world . besides , I feel it would be wrong to leave my father : I dread do wrong , for I think I might get wicked and hateful to myself , in the same way that many other seem hateful to I . for so long , so long I have never feel my outside world happy ; and if I get wicked I should lose my world of happy thought where my mother live with I . that be my childish notion all through those year . oh how long they be ! " 

 Mirah fall to muse again .
