 " have you no teaching about what be your duty ? " say Mrs. Meyrick . she do not like to say " religion " find herself on inspection rather dim as to what the Hebrew religion might have turn into at this date . 

 " no only that I ought to do what my father wish . he do not follow our religion at New York , and I think he want I not to know much about it . but because my mother use to take I to the synagogue , and I remember sit on her knee and look through the railing and hear the chanting and singing , I long to go . one day when I be quite small I slip out and try to find the synagogue , but I lose myself a long while till a peddler question I and take I home . my father , miss I , have be much in fear , and be very angry . I too have be so frightened at lose myself that it be long before I think of venture out again . but after Signora leave we we go to room where our landlady be a Jewess and observe her religion . I ask she to take I with she to the synagogue ; and I read in her prayer - book and Bible , and when I have money enough I ask she to buy I book of my own , for these book seem a close companionship with my mother : I know that she must have look at the very word and say they . in that way I have come to know a little of our religion , and the history of our people , besides piece together what I read in play and other book about Jews and jewess ; because I be sure my mother obey her religion . I have leave off ask my father about she . it be very dreadful to say it , but I begin to disbelieve he . I have find that he do not always tell the truth , and make promise without meaning to keep they ; and that raise my suspicion that my mother and brother be still alive though he have tell I they be dead . for in go over the past again as I get old and know more , I feel sure that my mother have be deceive , and have expect to see we back again after a very little while ; and my father take I on his knee and tell I that my mother and brother be both dead seem to I now but a bit of acting , to set my mind at rest . the cruelty of that falsehood sink into I , and I hat all untruth because of it . I write to my mother secretly : I know the street , Colman Street , where we live , and that it be not Blackfriars Bridge and the Coburg , and that our name be Cohen then , though my father call we Lapidoth , because , he say , it be a name of his forefather in Poland . I send my letter secretly ; but no answer come , and I think there be no hope for I . our life in America do not last much long . my father suddenly tell I we be to pack up and go to Hamburg , and I be rather glad . I hop we might get among a different sort of people , and I know German quite well some german play almost all by heart . my father speak it well than he speak English . I be thirteen then , and I seem to myself quite old I know so much , and yet so little . I think other child can not feel as I do . I have often wish that I have be drown when I be go away from my mother . but I set myself to obey and suffer : what else could I do ? one day when we be on our voyage , a new thought come into my mind . I be not very ill that time , and I keep on deck a good deal . my father act and sing and joke to amuse people on board , and I use often to hear remark about he . one day , when I be look at the sea and nobody take notice of I , I overhear a gentleman say , ' oh , he be one of those clever Jews a rascal , I should not   wonder . there be no race like they for cunning in the man and beauty in the woman . I wonder what market he mean that daughter for . ' when I hear this it dart into my mind that the unhappiness in my life come from my be a Jewess , and that always to the end the world would think slightly of I and that I must bear it , for I should be judge by that name ; and it comfort I to believe that my suffering be part of the affliction of my people , my part in the long song of mourning that have be go on through age and age . for if many of our race be wicked and make merry in their wickedness what be that but part of the affliction bear by the just among they , who be despise for the sin of their brother ? but you have not reject I . " 

 Mirah have change her tone in this last sentence , have suddenly reflect that at this moment she have reason not for complaint but for gratitude . 

 " and we will try to save you from be judge unjustly by other , my poor child , " say Mrs. Meyrick , who have now give up all attempt at go on with her work , and sit listen with fold hand and a face hardly less eager than Mab 's would have be . " go on , go on : tell I all . " 

 " after that we live in different town Hamburg and Vienna , the long . I begin to study singing again : and my father always get money about the theatre . I think he bring a good deal of money from America , I never know why we leave . for some time he be in great spirit about my singing , and he make I rehearse part and act continually . he look forward to my come out in the opera . but by - and - by it seem that my voice would never be strong enough it do not fulfill its promise . my master at Vienna say , ' do not   strain it far : it will never do for the public : it be gold , but a thread of gold dust . ' my father be bitterly disappointed : we be not so well off at that time . I think I have not quite tell you what I feel about my father . I know he be fond of I and mean to indulge I , and that make I afraid of hurt he ; but he always mistook what would please I and give I happiness . it be his nature to take everything lightly ; and I soon leave off ask he any question about thing that I care for much , because he always turn they off with a joke . he would even ridicule our own people ; and once when he have be imitate their movement and their tone in pray , only to make other laugh , I could not restrain myself for I always have an anger in my heart about my mother and when we be alone , I say , ' Father , you ought not to mimic our own people before Christians who mock they : would it not be bad if I mimic you , that they might mock you ? ' but he only shrug his shoulder and laugh and pinch my chin , and say , ' you could not   do it , my dear . " it be this way of turn off everything , that make a great wall between I and my father , and whatever I feel most I take the most care to hide from he . for there be some thing when they be laugh at I could not bear it : the world seem like a hell to I . be this world and all the life upon it only like a farce or a vaudeville , where you find no great meaning ? why then be there tragedy and grand opera , where man do difficult thing and choose to suffer ? I think it be silly to speak of all thing as a joke . and I see that his wish I to sing the great music , and part in grand opera , be only wish for what would fetch the great price . that hem in my gratitude for his affectionateness , and the tender feeling I have toward he be pity . yes , I do sometimes pity he . he have age and change . now he be no long so lively . I think he seem bad less good to other than to I . every now and then in the latter year his gaiety go away suddenly , and he would sit at home silent and gloomy ; or he would come in and fle himself down and sob , just as I have do myself when I have be in trouble . if I put my hand on his knee and say , ' what be the matter , father ? ' he would make no answer , but would draw my arm round his neck and put his arm round I and go on cry . there never come any confidence between we ; but oh , I be sorry for he . at those moment I know he must feel his life bitter , and I press my cheek against his head and pray . those moment be what most bind I to he ; and I use to think how much my mother once love he , else she would not have marry he . 

 " but soon there come the dreadful time . we have be at Pesth and we come back to Vienna . in spite of what my master Leo have say , my father get I an engagement , not at the opera , but to take singing part at a suburb theatre in Vienna . he have nothing to do with the theatre then ; I do not understand what he do , but I think he be continually at a gambling house , though he be careful always about take I to the theatre . I be very miserable . the play I act in be detestable to I . man come about we and want to talk to I : woman and man seem to look at I with a sneer smile ; it be no well than a fiery furnace . perhaps I make it bad than it be you do not   know that life : but the glare and the face , and my have to go on and act and sing what I hat , and then see people who come to stare at I behind the scene it be all so much bad than when I be a little girl . I go through with it ; I do it ; I have set my mind to obey my father and work , for I see nothing well that I could do . but I feel that my voice be get weak , and I know that my acting be not good except when it be not really act , but the part be one that I could be myself in , and some feeling within I carry I along . that be seldom . 

 " then , in the midst of all this , the news come to I one morning that my father have be take to prison , and he have send for I . he do not tell I the reason why he be there , but he order I to go to an address he give I , to see a Count who would be able to get he release . the address be to some public room where I be to ask for the Count , and beg he to come to my father . I find he , and recognize he as a gentleman whom I have see the other night for the first time behind the scene . that agitate I , for I remember his way of look at I and kiss my hand I think it be in mockery . but I deliver my errand , and he promise to go immediately to my father , who come home again that very evening , bring the Count with he . I now begin to feel a horrible dread of this man , for he worry I with his attention , his eye be always on I : I feel sure that whatever else there might be in his mind toward I , below it all there be scorn for the Jewess and the actress . and when he come to I the next day in the theatre and would put my shawl around I , a terror take hold of I ; I see that my father want I to look pleased . the Count be neither very young nor very old ; his hair and eye be pale ; he be tall and walk heavily , and his face be heavy and grave except when he look at I . he smile at I , and his smile go through I with horror : I could not tell why he be so much bad to I than other man . some feeling be like our hearing : they come as sound do , before we know their reason . my father talk to I about he when we be alone , and praise he say what a good friend he have be . I say nothing , because I suppose he have get my father out of prison . when the Count come again , my father leave the room . he ask I if I like be on the stage . I say no , I only act in obedience to my father . he always speak French , and call I ' petite ange ' and such thing , which I feel insulting . I know he mean to make love to I , and I have it firmly in my mind that a nobleman and one who be not a Jew could have no love for I that be not half contempt . but then he tell I that I need not act any long ; he wish I to visit he at his beautiful place , where I might be queen of everything . it be difficult to I to speak , I feel so shake with anger : I could only say , ' I would rather stay on the stage forever , ' and I leave he there . hurry out of the room I see my father saunter in the passage . my heart be crush . I go past he and lock myself up . it have sink into I that my father be in a conspiracy with that man against I . but the next day he persuade I to come out : he say that I have mistake everything , and he would explain : if I do not come out and act and fulfill my engagement , we should be ruin and he must starve . so I go on acting , and for a week or more the Count never come near I . my father change our lodging , and keep at home except when he go to the theatre with I . he begin one day to speak discouragingly of my acting , and say , I could never go on singing in public I should lose my voice I ought to think of my future , and not put my nonsensical feeling between I and my fortune . he say , ' what will you do ? you will be bring down to sing and beg at people 's door . you have have a splendid offer and ought to accept it . ' I could not speak : a horror take possession of I when I think of my mother and of he . I feel for the first time that I should not do wrong to leave he . but the next day he tell I that he have put an end to my engagement at the theatre , and that we be to go to Prague . I be get suspicious of everything , and my will be harden to act against he . it take we two day to pack and get ready ; and I have it in my mind that I might be oblige to run away from my father , and then I would come to London and try if it be possible to find my mother . I have a little money , and I sell some thing to get more . I pack a few clothe in a little bag that I could carry with I , and I keep my mind on the watch . my father 's silence his letting drop that subject of the Count 's offer make I feel sure that there be a plan against I . I feel as if it have be a plan to take I to a madhouse . I once see a picture of a madhouse , that I could never forget ; it seem to I very much like some of the life I have see the people strut , quarrel , leer the face with cunning and malice in they . it be my will to keep myself from wickedness ; and I pray for help . I have see what despise woman be : and my heart turn against my father , for I see always behind he that man who make I shudder . you will think I have not enough reason for my suspicion , and perhaps I have not , outside my own feeling ; but it seem to I that my mind have be light up , and all that might be stand out clear and sharp . if I sleep , it be only to see the same sort of thing , and I could hardly sleep at all . through our journey I be everywhere on the watch . I do not   know why , but it come before I like a real event , that my father would suddenly leave I and I should find myself with the Count where I could not get away from he . I think God be warn I : my mother 's voice be in my soul . it be dark when we reach Prague , and though the strange bunch of lamp be light it be difficult to distinguish face as we drive along the street . my father choose to sit outside he be always smoke now and I watch everything in spite of the darkness . I do believe I could see well then than I ever do before : the strange clearness within seem to have get outside I . it be not my habit to notice face and figure much in the street ; but this night I see every one ; and when we pass before a great hotel I catch sight only of a back that be pass in the light of the great bunch of lamp a good way off fall on it . I know it before the face be turn , as it fall into shadow , I know who it be . help come to I . I feel sure help come . I do not sleep that night . I put on my plainest thing the cloak and hat I have wear ever since ; and I sit watch for the light and the sound of the door be unbarred . some one rise early at four o'clock , to go to the railway . that give I courage . I slip out , with my little bag under my cloak , and none notice I . I have be a long while attend to the railway guide that I might learn the way to England ; and before the sun have rise I be in the train for Dresden . then I cry for joy . I do not know whether my money would last out , but I trust . I could sell the thing in my bag , and the little ring in my ear , and I could live on bread only . my only terror be lest my father should follow I . but I never pause . I come on , and on , and on , only eat bread now and then . when I get to Brussels I see that I should not have enough money , and I sell all that I could sell ; but here a strange thing happen . put my hand into the pocket of my cloak , I find a half - napoleon . wonder and wonder how it come there , I remember that on the way from Cologne there be a young workman sit against I . I be frightened at every one , and do not like to be speak to . at first he try to talk , but when he see that I do not like it , he leave off . it be a long journey ; I eat nothing but a bit of bread , and he once offer I some of the food he bring in , but I refuse it . I do believe it be he who put that bit of gold in my pocket . without it I could hardly have get to Dover , and I do walk a good deal of the way from Dover to London . I know I should look like a miserable beggar - girl . I want not to look very miserable , because if I find my mother it would grieve she to see I so . but oh , how vain my hope be that she would be there to see I come ! as soon as I set foot in London , I begin to ask for Lambeth and Blackfriars Bridge , but they be a long way off , and I go wrong . at last I get to Blackfriars Bridge and ask for Colman Street . People shake their head . none know it . I see it in my mind our doorstep , and the white tile hang in the window , and the large brick building opposite with wide door . but there be nothing like it . at last when I ask a tradesman where the Coburg Theatre and Colman Street be , he say , ' oh , my little woman , that be all do away with . the old street have be pull down ; everything be new . ' I turn away and feel as if death have lay a hand on I . he say : ' stop , stop ! young woman ; what be it you be want with Colman Street , eh ? ' mean well , perhaps . but his tone be what I could not bear ; and how could I tell he what I want ? I feel blinded and bewilder with a sudden shock . I suddenly feel that I be very weak and weary , and yet where could I go ? for I look so poor and dusty , and have nothing with I I look like a street - beggar . and I be afraid of all place where I could enter . I lose my trust . I think I be forsake . it seem that I have be in a fever of hope deliriou all the way from Prague : I think that I be help , and I do nothing but strain my mind forward and think of find my mother ; and now there I stand in a strange world . all who see I would think ill of I , and I must herd with beggar . I stand on the bridge and look along the river . People be go on to a steamboat . many of they seem poor , and I feel as if it would be a refuge to get away from the street ; perhaps the boat would take I where I could soon get into a solitude . I have still some penny leave , and I buy a loaf when I go on the boat . I want to have a little time and strength to think of life and death . how could I live ? and now again it seem that if ever I be to find my mother again , death be the way to she . I eat , that I might have strength to think . the boat set I down at a place along the river I do not   know where and it be late in the evening . I find some large tree apart from the road , and I sit down under they that I might rest through the night . sleep must have soon come to I , and when I awake it be morning . the bird be sing , and the dew be white about I , I feel chill and oh , so lonely ! I get up and walk and follow the river a long way and then turn back again . there be no reason why I should go anywhere . the world about I seem like a vision that be hurry by while I stand still with my pain . my thought be strong than I be ; they rush in and force I to see all my life from the beginning ; ever since I be carry away from my mother I have feel myself a lost child take up and use by stranger , who do not care what my life be to I , but only what I could do for they . it seem all a weary wandering and heart - loneliness as if I have be force to go to merrymaking without the expectation of joy . and now it be bad . I be lose again , and I dread lest any stranger should notice I and speak to I . I have a terror of the world . none know I ; all would mistake I . I have see so many in my life who make themselves glad with scorning , and laugh at another 's shame . what could I do ? this life seem to be close in upon I with a wall of fire everywhere there be scorch that make I shrink . the high sunlight make I shrink . and I begin to think that my despair be the voice of God tell I to die . but it would take I long to die of hunger . then I think of my people , how they have be drive from land to land and be afflict , and multitude have die of misery in their wandering be I the first ? and in the war and trouble when Christians be cruel , our father have sometimes slay their child and afterward themselves : it be to save they from be false apostate . that seem to make it right for I to put an end to my life ; for calamity have close I in too , and I see no pathway but to evil . but my mind get into war with itself , for there be contrary thing in it . I know that some have hold it wrong to hasten their own death , though they be in the midst of flame ; and while I have some strength leave it be a longing to bear if I ought to bear else where be the good of all my life ? it have not be happy since the first year : when the light come every morning I use to think , ' I will bear it . ' but always before I have some hope ; now it be go . with these thought I wander and wander , inwardly cry to the most High , from whom I should not flee in death more than in life though I have no strong faith that he care for I . the strength seem depart from my soul ; deep below all my cry be the feeling that I be alone and forsake . the more I think the weary I get , till it seem I be not think at all , but only the sky and the river and the eternal God be in my soul . and what be it whether I die or live ? if I lie down to die in the river , be it more than lie down to sleep ? for there too I commit my soul I give myself up . I could not bear memory any more ; I could only feel what be present in I it be all one longing to cease from my weary life , which seem only a pain outside the great peace that I might enter into . that be how it be . when the evening come and the sun be go , it seem as if that be all I have to wait for . and a new strength come into I to will what I would do . you know what I do . I be go to die . you know what happen do he not tell you ? faith come to I again ; I be not forsake . he tell you how he find I ? " 

 Mrs. Meyrick give no audible answer , but press her lip against 
 Mirah 's forehead . 
         

 " she be just a pearl ; the mud have only wash she , " be the fervid little woman 's closing commentary when , tete - a - tete with Deronda in the back parlor that evening , she have convey Mirah 's story to he with much vividness . 

 " what be your feeling about a search for this mother ? " say Deronda . 
 " have you no fear ? I have , I confess . " 
 " oh , I believe the mother 's good , " say Mrs. Meyrick , with rapid decisiveness ; " or be good . she may be dead that be my fear . a good woman , you may depend : you may know it by the scoundrel the father be . where do the child get her goodness from ? wheaten flour have to be account for . " 

 Deronda be rather disappointed at this answer ; he have want a confirmation of his own judgment , and he begin to put in demurrer . the argument about the mother would not apply to the brother ; and Mrs. Meyrick admit that the brother might be an ugly likeness of the father . then , as to advertising , if the name be Cohen , you might as well advertise for two undescribed terrier ; and here Mrs. Meyrick help he , for the idea of an advertisement , already mention to Mirah , have rouse the poor child 's terror ; she be convince that her father would see it he see everything in the paper . certainly there be safe mean than advertising ; man might be set to work whose business it be to find missing person ; but Deronda wish Mrs. Meyrick to feel with he that it would be wise to wait , before seek a dubious perhaps a deplorable result ; especially as he be engage to go abroad the next week for a couple of month . if a search be make , he would like to be at hand , so that Mrs. Meyrick might not be unaide in meet any consequence suppose that she would generously continue to watch over Mirah . 

 " we should be very jealous of any one who take the task from we , " say Mrs. Meyrick . " she will stay under my roof ; there be Hans 's old room for she . " 

 " will she be content to wait ? " say Deronda , anxiously . 

 " no trouble there . it be not her nature to run into planning and devising : only to submit . see how she submit to that father ! it be a wonder to herself how she find the will and contrivance to run away from he . about find her mother , her only notion now be to trust ; since you be send to save she and we be good to she , she trust that her mother will be find in the same unsought way . and when she be talk I catch her feeling like a child . " 

 Mrs. Meyrick hop that the sum Deronda put into her hand as a provision for Mirah 's want be more than would be need ; after a little while Mirah would perhaps like to occupy herself as the other girl do , and make herself independent . Deronda plead that she must need a long rest . " oh , yes ; we will hurry nothing , " say Mrs. Meyrick . 

 " rely upon it , she shall be take tender care of . if you like to give I your address abroad , I will write to let you know how we get on . it be not fair that we should have all the pleasure of her salvation to ourselves . and besides , I want to make believe that I be do something for you as well as for Mirah . " 

 " that be no make - believe . what should I have do without you last night ? everything would have go wrong . I shall tell Hans that the good of have he for a friend be , know his mother . " 

 after that they join the girl in the other room , where Mirah be seat placidly , while the other be tell she what they know about Mr. Deronda his goodness to Hans , and all the virtue that Hans have report of he . 

 " Kate burn a pastille before his portrait every day , " say Mab . " and I carry his signature in a little black - silk bag round my neck to keep off the cramp . and Amy say the multiplication - table in his name . we must all do something extra in honor of he , now he have bring you to we . " 

 " I suppose he be too great a person to want anything , " say Mirah , smile at Mab , and appeal to the graver Amy . " he be perhaps very high in the world ? " 

 " he be very much above we in rank , " say Amy . " he be relate to grand people . I dare say he lean on some of the satin cushion we prick our finger over . " 

 " I be glad he be of high rank , " say Mirah , with her usual quietness . 

 " now , why be you glad of that ? " say Amy , rather suspicious of this sentiment , and on the watch for jewish peculiarity which have not appear . 

 " because I have always dislike man of high rank before . " 

 " oh , Mr. Deronda be not so very high , " say Kate , " he need not hinder we from think ill of the whole peerage and baronetage if we like . " 

 when he enter , Mirah rise with the same look of grateful reverence that she have lift to he the evening before : impossible to see a creature free at once from embarrassment and boldness . her theatrical training have leave no recognizable trace ; probably her manner have not much change since she play the forsaken child at nine year of age ; and she have grow up in her simplicity and truthfulness like a little flower - seed that absorb the chance confusion of its surrounding into its own definite mould of beauty . Deronda feel that he be make acquaintance with something quite new to he in the form of womanhood . for Mirah be not childlike from ignorance : her experience of evil and trouble be deep and strange than his own . he feel inclined to watch she and listen to she as if she have come from a far off shore inhabit by a race different from our own . 

 but for that very reason he make his visit brief with his usual activity of imagination as to how his conduct might affect other , he shrink from what might seem like curiosity or the assumption of a right to know as much as he please of one to whom he have do a service . for example , he would have like to hear her sing , but he would have feel the expression of such a wish to be rudeness in he since she could not refuse , and he would all the while have a sense that she be be treat like one whose accomplishment be to be ready on demand . and whatever reverence could be show to woman , he be bent on show to this girl . why ? he give himself several good reason ; but whatever one do with a strong unhesitating outflow of will have a store of motive that it would be hard to put into word . some deed seem little more than interjection which give vent to the long passion of a life . 

 so Deronda soon take his farewell for the two month during which he expect to be absent from London , and in a few day he be on his way with Sir Hugo and Lady Mallinger to Leubronn . 

 he have fulfil his intention of tell they about Mirah . the baronet be decidedly of opinion that the search for the mother and brother have well be let alone . Lady Mallinger be much interested in the poor girl , observe that there be a society for the conversion of the Jews , and that it be to be hop Mirah would embrace Christianity ; but perceive that Sir Hugo look at she with amusement , she conclude that she have say something foolish . Lady Mallinger feel apologetically about herself as a woman who have produce nothing but daughter in a case where son be require , and hence regard the apparent contradiction of the world as probably due to the weakness of her own understanding . but when she be much puzzle , it be her habit to say to herself , " I will ask Daniel . " Deronda be altogether a convenience in the family ; and Sir Hugo too , after intend to do the good for he , have begin to feel that the pleasant result would be to have this substitute for a son always ready at his elbow . 

 this be the history of Deronda , so far as he know it , up to the time of that visit to Leubronn in which he see Gwendolen Harleth at the gaming - table . 

 CHAPTER XXI . 

 it be a common sentence that Knowledge be power ; but who hath duly consider or set forth the power of ignorance ? Knowledge slowly build up what ignorance in an hour pull down . Knowledge , through patient and frugal century , enlarge discovery and make record of it ; ignorance , want its day 's dinner , light a fire with the record , and give a flavor to its one roast with the burn soul of many generation . Knowledge , instruct the sense , refining and multiplying need , transform itself into skill and make life various with a new six day ' work ; come Ignorance drunk on the seventh , with a firkin of oil and a match and an easy " let there not be , " and the many - color creation be shrivel up in blackness . of a truth , Knowledge be power , but it be a power rein by scruple , have a conscience of what must be and what may be ; whereas ignorance be a blind giant who , let he but wax unbound , would make it a sport to seize the pillar that hold up the long - wrought fabric of human good , and turn all the place of joy dark as a bury Babylon . and look at life parcel - wise , in the growth of a single lot , who have a practiced vision may not see that ignorance of the true bond between event , and false conceit of means whereby sequence may be compel like that falsity of eyesight which overlook the gradation of distance , see that which be afar off as if it be within a step or a grasp precipitate the mistaken soul on destruction ? 

 it be half - past ten in the morning when Gwendolen Harleth , after her gloomy journey from Leubronn , arrive at the station from which she must drive to Offendene . no carriage or friend be await she , for in the telegram she have send from Dover she have mention a later train , and in her impatience of linger at a London station she have set off without picture what it would be to arrive unannounced at half an hour 's drive from home at one of those station which have be fix on not as near anywhere , but as equidistant from everywhere . deposit as a femme sole with her large trunk , and have to wait while a vehicle be be get from the large - sized lantern call the Railway Inn , Gwendolen feel that the dirty paint in the waiting - room , the dusty decanter of flat water , and the text in large letter call on she to repent and be convert , be part of the dreary prospect open by her family trouble ; and she hurry away to the outer door look toward the lane and field . but here the very gleam of sunshine seem melancholy , for the autumnal leaf and grass be shiver , and the wind be turn up the feather of a cock and two croaking hen which have doubtless part with their grow - up offspring and do not know what to do with themselves . the railway official also seem without resource , and his innocent demeanor in observe Gwendolen and her trunk be render intolerable by the cast in his eye ; especially since , be a new man , he do not know she , and must conclude that she be not very high in the world . the vehicle a dirty old barouche be within sight , and be be slowly prepare by an elderly laborer . contemptible detail these , to make part of a history ; yet the turn of most life be hardly to be account for without they . they be continually enter with cumulative force into a mood until it get the mass and momentum of a theory or a motive . even philosophy be not quite free from such determine influence ; and to be drop solitary at an ugly , irrelevant - looking spot , with a sense of no income on the mind , might well prompt a man to discourage speculation on the origin of thing and the reason of a world where a subtle thinker find himself so badly off . how much more might such trifle tell on a young lady equip for society with a fastidious taste , an indian shawl over her arm , some twenty cubic foot of trunk by her side , and a mortal dislike to the new consciousness of poverty which be stimulate her imagination of disagreeable ? at any rate they tell heavily on poor Gwendolen , and help to quell her resistant spirit . what be the good of live in the midst of hardship , ugliness , and humiliation ? this be the beginning of be at home again , and it be a sample of what she have to expect . 

 here be the theme on which her discontent ring its sad change during her slow drive in the uneasy barouche , with one great trunk squeeze the meek driver , and the other fasten with a rope on the seat in front of she . her rule vision all the way from Leubronn have be that the family would go abroad again ; for of course there must be some little income leave her mamma do not mean that they would have literally nothing . to go to a dull place abroad and live poorly , be the dismal future that threaten she : she have see plenty of poor english people abroad and imagine herself plunge in the despise dullness of their ill - plenishe life , with Alice , Bertha , Fanny and Isabel all grow up in tediousness around she , while she advance toward thirty and her mamma get more and more melancholy . but she do not mean to submit , and let misfortune do what it would with she : she have not yet quite believe in the misfortune ; but weariness and disgust with this wretched arrival have begin to affect she like an uncomfortable waking , bad than the uneasy dream which have go before . the self - delight with which she have kiss her image in the glass have fade before the sense of futility in be anything whatever charming , clever , resolute what be the good of it all ? event might turn out anyhow , and man be hateful . yes , man be hateful . but in these last hour , a certain change have come over their meaning . it be one thing to hate steal good , and another thing to hate they the more because their be steal hinder we from make use of they . Gwendolen have begin to be angry with Grandcourt for be what have hinder she from marry he , angry with he as the cause of her present dreary lot . 

 but the slow drive be nearly at an end , and the lumber vehicle come up the avenue be within sight of the window . a figure appear under the portico bring a rush of new and less selfish feeling in Gwendolen , and when spring from the carriage she see the dear beautiful face with fresh line of sadness in it , she throw her arm round her mother 's neck , and for the moment feel all sorrow only in relation to her mother 's feeling about they . 

 behind , of course , be the sad face of the four superfluous girl , each , poor thing like those other many thousand sister of we all have her peculiar world which be of no importance to any one else , but all of they feel Gwendolen 's presence to be somehow a relenting of misfortune : where Gwendolen be , something interesting would happen ; even her hurried submission to their kiss , and " now go away , girl , " carry the sort of comfort which all weakness find in decision and authoritativeness . good Miss Merry , whose air of meek depression , hitherto hold unaccountable in a governess affectionately attach to the family , be now at the general level of circumstance , do not expect any greeting , but busy herself with the trunk and the coachman 's pay ; while Mrs. Davilow and Gwendolen hasten up - stair and shut themselves in the black and yellow bedroom . 

 " never mind , mamma dear , " say Gwendolen , tenderly press her handkerchief against the tear that be roll down Mrs. Davilow 's cheek . " never mind . I do not   mind . I will do something . I will be something . thing will come right . it seem bad because I be away . come now ! you must be glad because I be here . " 

 Gwendolen feel every word of that speech . a rush of compassionate tenderness stir all her capability of generous resolution ; and the self - confident project which have vaguely glance before she during her journey spring instantaneously into new definiteness . suddenly she seem to perceive how she could be ' something . I and the girl be go to work a table - cloth border for the Ladies ' charity at Winchester , and a communion cloth that the parishioner be to present to Pennicote Church . "

 the ebb of sympathetic care for her mamma have go so low just now , that Gwendolen take no notice of these deprecatory word .

I have nearly two hundred pound , and it would have do for we to live on a little while , till I could carry out some plan . " she pause an instant and then add more impetuously , " everything have go against I . People have come near I only to blight I . " 

 among the " people " she be include Deronda . if he have not interfere in her life she would have go to the gaming - table again with a few napoleon , and might have win back her loss . 

 " we must resign ourselves to the will of Providence , my child , " say poor Mrs. Davilow , startle by this revelation of the gambling , but not dare to say more . she feel sure that " people " mean Grandcourt , about whom her lip be seal . and Gwendolen answer immediately 

 " but I do not   resign myself . I shall do what I can against it . what be the good of call the people 's wickedness providence ? you say in your letter it be Mr. Lassman 's fault we have lose our money . have he run away with it all ? " 

 " no , dear , you do not   understand . there be great speculation : he mean to gain . it be all about mine and thing of that sort . he risk too much . "

 " my dear child , law can never bring back money lose in that way . your uncle say it be milk spill upon the ground . besides , one must have a fortune to get any law : there be no law for people who be ruin . and our money have only go along with other 's people 's . we be not the only sufferer : other have to resign themselves besides we . " 

 " but I do not   resign myself to live at Sawyer 's cottage and see you work for sixpence and shilling because of that . I shall not do it . I shall do what be more befit our rank and education . "

 " I think , myself , that Dr. Monpert 's would be more suitable . there could be no hardship in a bishop 's family . "

Fancy I shut up with three awkward girl something like Alice ! I would rather emigrate than be a governess . "

Mrs. Davilow be mute , see no outlet , and think with dread of the collision that might happen when Gwendolen have to meet her uncle and aunt . there be an air of reticence in Gwendolen 's haughty , resistant speech which imply that she have a definite plan in reserve ; and her practical ignorance continually exhibit , could not nullify the mother 's belief in the effectiveness of that forcible will and daring which have hold mastery over herself .

Jocosa be go to leave we , of course . but she might do that first . " 

 " she would do anything she could , poor , dear soul . I have not tell you yet she want I to take all her saving her three hundred pound . I tell she to set up a little school . it will be hard for she to go into a new family now she have be so long with we . "

 " he will be hurt at your despise what he have exert himself about . 
 but I dare say you have something else in your mind that he might not 
 disapprove , if you consult he . "

Miss Arrowpoint leave her card the other day : I could not see she . but I do not   know about Herr Klesmer . do you want to send before to - morrow ? "

" but mind you do not   contradict I or put hindrance in my way . I must decide for myself . I can not be dictate to by my uncle or any one else . my life be my own affair . and I think " here her tone take an edge of scorn " I think I can do well for you than let you live in Sawyer 's cottage . " 

 in utter this last sentence Gwendolen again rise , and go to a desk where she write the follow note to Klesmer : 

 Miss Harleth present her compliment to Herr Klesmer , and venture to request of he the very great favor that he will call upon she , if possible , to - morrow . her reason for presume so far on his kindness be of a very serious nature . unfortunate family circumstance have oblige she to take a course in which she can only turn for advice to the great knowledge and judgment of Herr Klesmer .

 for the moment , the absorbing purpose be to get the letter dispatch ; but when she have be assure on this point , another anxiety arise and keep she in a state of uneasy excitement . if Klesmer happen not to be at Quetcham , what could she do next ? Gwendolen 's belief in her star , so to speak , have have some bruise . thing have go against she . a splendid marriage which present itself within reach have show a hideous flaw . the chance of roulette have not adjust themselves to her claim ; and a man of whom she know nothing have thrust himself between she and her intention . the conduct of those unintereste people who manage the business of the world have be culpable just in the point most injurious to she in particular . Gwendolen Harleth , with all her beauty and conscious force , feel the close threat of humiliation : for the first time the condition of this world seem to she like a hurrying roar crowd in which she have get astray , no more cared for and protect than a myriad of other girl , in spite of its be a peculiar hardship to she . if Klesmer be not at Quetcham that would be all of a piece with the rest : the unwelcome negative urge itself as a probability , and set her brain work at desperate alternative which might deliver she from Sawyer 's Cottage or the ultimate necessity of " take a situation , " a phrase that sum up for she the disagreeable most wound to her pride , most irksome to her taste ; at least so far as her experience enable she to imagine disagreeable . 

 still Klesmer might be there , and Gwendolen think of the result in that case with a hopefulness which even cast a satisfactory light over her peculiar trouble , as what might well enter into the biography of celebrity and remarkable person . and if she have hear her immediate acquaintance cross - examine as to whether they think her remarkable , the first who say " no " would have surprise she . 

 CHAPTER XXII . 

  we please our fancy with ideal webs 
  of innovation , but our life meanwhile 
  be in the loom , where busy passion ply 
  the shuttle to and fro , and give our deed 
  the accustomed pattern . 
 Gwendolen 's note , come " pat betwixt too early and too late , " be put into Klesmer 's hand just when he be leave Quetcham , and in order to meet her appeal to his kindness he , with some inconvenience to himself spend the night at Wanchester . there be reason why he would not remain at Quetcham . 

 that magnificent mansion , fit with regard to the great expense , have in fact become too hot for he , its owner have , like some great politician , be astonish at an insurrection against the establish order of thing , which we plain people after the event can perceive to have be prepare under their very nose . 

 there be as usual many guest in the house , and among they one in whom Miss Arrowpoint foresee a new pretender to her hand : a political man of good family who confidently expect a peerage , and feel on public ground that he require a large fortune to support the title properly . heiress vary , and person interested in one of they beforehand be prepared to find that she be too yellow or too red , tall and topple or short and square , violent and capricious or moony and insipid ; but in every case it be take for grant that she will consider herself an appendage to her fortune , and marry where other think her fortune ought to go . nature , however , not only accommodate herself ill to our favorite practice by make " only child " daughter , but also now and then endow the misplaced daughter with a clear head and a strong will . the Arrowpoints have already feel some anxiety owe to these endowment of their Catherine . she would not accept the view of her social duty which require she to marry a needy nobleman or a commoner on the ladder toward nobility ; and they be not without uneasiness concern her persistence in decline suitable offer . as to the possibility of she be in love with Klesmer they be not at all uneasy a very common sort of blindness . for in general mortal have a great power of be astonish at the presence of an effect toward which they have do everything , and at the absence of an effect toward which they have do nothing but desire it . parent be astonish at the ignorance of their son , though they have use the most time - honor and expensive mean of secure it ; husband and wife be mutually astonish at the loss of affection which they have take no pain to keep ; and all of we in our turn be apt to be astonish that our neighbor do not admire we . in this way it happen that the truth seem highly improbable . the truth be something different from the habitual lazy combination beget by our wish . the arrowpoint ' hour of astonishment be come . 

 when there be a passion between an heiress and a proud independent - spirited man , it be difficult for they to come to an understanding ; but the difficulty be likely to be overcome unless the proud man secure himself by a constant alibi . brief meeting after studied absence be potent in disclosure : but more potent still be frequent companionship , with full sympathy in taste and admirable quality on both side ; especially where the one be in the position of teacher and the other be delightedly conscious of receptive ability which also give the teacher delight . the situation be famous in history , and have no less charm now than it have in the day of Abelard . 

 but this kind of comparison have not occur to the Arrowpoints when they first engage Klesmer to come down to Quetcham . to have a first - rate musician in your house be a privilege of wealth ; Catherine 's musical talent demand every advantage ; and she particularly desire to use her quieter time in the country for more thorough study . Klesmer be not yet a Liszt , understand to be adore by lady of all european country with the exception of Lapland : and even with that understanding it do not follow that he would make proposal to an heiress . no musician of honor would do so . still less be it conceivable that Catherine would give he the slight pretext for such daring . the large check that Mr. Arrowpoint be to draw in Klesmer 's name seem to make he as safe an inmate as a footman . where marriage be inconceivable , a girl 's sentiment be safe . 

 Klesmer be eminently a man of honor , but marriage rarely begin with formal proposal , and moreover , Catherine 's limit of the conceivable do not exactly correspond with her mother 's . 

 outsider might have be more apt to think that Klesmer 's position be dangerous for himself if Miss Arrowpoint have be an acknowledge beauty ; not take into account that the most powerful of all beauty be that which reveal itself after sympathy and not before it . there be a charm of eye and lip which come with every little phrase that certify delicate perception or fine judgment , with every unostentatious word or smile that show a heart awake to other ; and no sweep of garment or turn of figure be more satisfying than that which enter as a restoration of confidence that one person be present on whom no intention will be lose . what dignity of meaning go on gather in frown and laugh which be never observe in the wrong place ; what suffuse adorableness in a human frame where there be a mind that can flash out comprehension and hand that can execute finely ! the more obvious beauty , also adorable sometimes one may say it without blasphemy begin by be an apology for folly , and end like other apology in become tiresome by iteration ; and that Klesmer , though very susceptible to it , should have a passionate attachment to Miss Arrowpoint , be no more a paradox than any other triumph of a manifold sympathy over a monotonous attraction . we object less to be tax with the enslave excess of our passion than with our deficiency in wide passion ; but if the truth be know , our reputed intensity be often the dullness of not know what else to do with ourselves . Tannhšuser , one suspect , be a knight of ill - furnish imagination , hardly of large discourse than a heavy guardsman ; Merlin have certainly see his good day , and be merely repeat himself , when he fall into that hopeless captivity ; and we know that Ulysses feel so manifest an ennui under similar circumstance that Calypso herself further his departure . there be indeed a report that he afterward leave Penelope ; but since she be habitually absorb in worsted work , and it be probably from she that Telemachus get his mean , pettifog disposition , always anxious about the property and the daily consumption of meat , no inference can be draw from this already dubious scandal as to the relation between companionship and constancy . 

 Klesmer be as versatile and fascinating as a young Ulysses on a sufficient acquaintance one whom nature seem to have first make generously and then to have add music as a dominant power use all the abundant rest , and , as in Mendelssohn , find expression for itself not only in the high finish of execution , but in that fervor of creative work and theoretic belief which pierce devoted purpose . his foible of arrogance and vanity do not exceed such as may be find in the good english family ; and Catherine Arrowpoint have no corresponding restlessness to clash with his : notwithstanding her native kindliness she be perhaps too coolly firm and self - sustain . but she be one of those satisfactory creature whose intercourse have the charm of discovery ; whose integrity of faculty and expression beget a wish to know what they will say on all subject or how they will perform whatever they undertake ; so that they end by raise not only a continual expectation but a continual sense of fulfillment the systole and diastole of blissful companionship . in such case the outward presentment easily become what the image be to the worshipper . it be not long before the two become aware that each be interesting to the other ; but the " how far " remain a matter of doubt . Klesmer do not conceive that Miss Arrowpoint be likely to think of he as a possible lover , and she be not accustomed to think of herself as likely to stir more than a friendly regard , or to fear the expression of more from any man who be not enamored of her fortune . each be content to suffer some unshared sense of denial for the sake of love the other 's society a little too well ; and under these condition no need have be feel to restrict Klesmer 's visit for the last year either in country or in town . he know very well that if Miss Arrowpoint have be poor he would have make ardent love to she instead of send a storm through the piano , or fold his arm and pour out a hyperbolical tirade about something as impersonal as the north pole ; and she be not less aware that if it have be possible for Klesmer to wish for her hand she would have find overmaster reason for give it to he . here be the safety of full cup , which be as secure from overflow as the half - empty , always suppose no disturbance . naturally , silent feeling have not remain at the same point any more than the stealthly dial - hand , and in the present visit to Quetcham , Klesmer have begin to think that he would not come again ; while Catherine be more sensitive to his frequent brusquerie , which she rather resent as a needless effort to assert his footing of superior in every sense except the conventional . 

 meanwhile enter the expectant peer , Mr. Bult , an esteemed party man who , rather neutral in private life , have strong opinion concern the district of the Niger , be much at home also in Brazils , speak with decision of affair in the South Seas , be studious of his parliamentary and itinerant speech , and have the general solidity and suffusive pinkness of a healthy Briton on the central table - land of life . Catherine , aware of a tacit understanding that he be an undeniable husband for an heiress , have nothing to say against he but that he be thoroughly tiresome to she . Mr. Bult be amiably confident , and have no idea that his insensibility to counterpoint could ever be reckon against he . Klesmer he hardly regard in the light of a serious human being who ought to have a vote ; and he do not mind Miss Arrowpoint 's addiction to music any more than her probable expense in antique lace . he be consequently a little amazed at an after - dinner outburst of Klesmer 's on the lack of idealism in english politic , which leave all mutuality between distant race to be determine simply by the need of a market ; the crusade , to his mind , have at least this excuse , that they have a banner of sentiment round which generous feeling could rally : of course , the scoundrel rally too , but what then ? they rally in equal force round your advertisement van of " buy cheap , sell dear . " on this theme Klesmer 's eloquence , gesticulatory and other , go on for a little while like stray firework accidentally ignite , and then sink into immovable silence . Mr. Bult be not surprised that Klesmer 's opinion should be flighty , but be astonish at his command of english idiom and his ability to put a point in a way that would have tell at a constituent ' dinner to be account for probably by his be a Pole , or a Czech , or something of that ferment sort , in a state of political refugeeism which have oblige he to make a profession of his music ; and that evening in the drawing - room he for the first time go up to Klesmer at the piano , Miss Arrowpoint be near , and say 

 " I have no idea before that you be a political man . " 

 Klesmer 's only answer be to fold his arm , put out his nether lip , and stare at Mr. Bult . 

 " you must have be use to public speaking . you speak uncommonly well , though I do not   agree with you . from what you say about sentiment , I fancy you be a Panslavist . " 

 " no ; my name be Elijah . I be the Wandering Jew , " say Klesmer , flash a smile at Miss Arrowpoint , and suddenly make a mysterious , wind - like rush backward and forward on the piano . Mr. Bult feel this buffoonery rather offensive and polish , but Miss Arrowpoint be there do not like to move away . 

 " Herr Klesmer have cosmopolitan idea , " say Miss Arrowpoint , try to make the good of the situation . " he look forward to a fusion of race . " 

 " with all my heart , " say Mr. Bult , willing to be gracious . " I be sure he have too much talent to be a mere musician . " 

 " ah , sir , you be under some mistake there , " say Klesmer , fire up . " no man have too much talent to be a musician . Most man have too little . a creative artist be no more a mere musician than a great statesman be a mere politician . we be not ingenious puppet , sir , who live in a box and look out on the world only when it be gap for amusement . we help to rule the nation and make the age as much as any other public man . we count ourselves on level bench with legislator . and a man who speak effectively through music be compel to something more difficult than parliamentary eloquence . " 

 with the last word Klesmer wheel from the piano and walk away . 

 Miss Arrowpoint color , and Mr. Bult observe , with his usual phlegmatic stolidity , " your pianist do not think small beer of himself . " 

 " Herr Klesmer be something more than a pianist , " say Miss Arrowpoint , apologetically . " he be a great musician in the full sense of the word . he will rank with Schubert and Mendelssohn . " 

 " ah , you lady understand these thing , " say Mr. Bult , none the less convinced that these thing be frivolous because Klesmer have show himself a coxcomb . 

 Catherine , always sorry when Klesmer give himself air , find an opportunity the next day in the music - room to say , " why be you so heated last night with Mr. Bult ? he mean no harm . " 

 " you wish I to be complaisant to he ? " say Klesmer , rather fiercely . 

 " I think it be hardly worth your while to be other than civil . " 

 " you find no difficulty in tolerate he , then ? you have a respect for a political platitudinarian as insensible as an ox to everything he can not   turn into political capital . you think his monumental obtuseness suit to the dignity of the english gentleman . " 

 " I do not say that . " 

 " you mean that I act without dignity , and you be offend with I . " 

 " now you be slightly near the truth , " say Catherine , smile . 

 " then I have well put my burial - clothe in my portmanteau and set off at once . " 

 " I do not   see that . if I have to bear your criticism of my operetta , you should not mind my criticism of your impatience . " 

 " but I do mind it . you would have wish I to take his ignorant impertinence about a ' mere musician ' without let he know his place . I be to hear my god blaspheme as well as myself insult . but I beg pardon . it be impossible you should see the matter as I do . even you can not   understand the wrath of the artist : he be of another caste for you . " 

 " that be true , " say Catherine , with some betrayal of feel . " he be of a caste to which I look up a caste above mine . " 

 Klesmer , who have be seat at a table look over score , start up and walk to a little distance , from which he say 

 " that be finely feel I be grateful . but I have well go , all the same . I have make up my mind to go , for good and all . you can get on exceedingly well without I : your operetta be on wheel it will go of itself . and your Mr. Bull 's company fit I ' wie die Faust in Auge . ' I be neglect my engagement . I must go off to St. Petersburg . " 

 there be no answer .

I have only to wonder that you have consent to give we so much of your time in the last year . there must be treble the interest to you anywhere else . I have never think of you consent to come here as anything else than a sacrifice . " 

 " why should I make the sacrifice ? " say Klesmer , go to seat himself at the piano , and touch the key so as to give with the delicacy of an echo in the far distance a melody which he have set to Heine 's " Ich hab ' dich geliebet und liebe dich noch . "

 " none that seem in the least probable . " 

 " then I shall tell you . it be because you be to I the chief woman in the world the throne lady whose color I carry between my heart and my armor . " 

 Catherine 's hand tremble so much that she could no long tear the paper : still less could her lip utter a word . Klesmer go on 

 " this would be the last impertinence in I , if I mean to found anything upon it . that be out of the question . I mean no such thing . but you once say it be your doom to suspect every man who court you of be an adventurer , and what make you angriest be man 's impute to you the folly of believe that they court you for your own sake . do you not say so ? "

 " it be a bitter word . well , at least one man who have see woman as plenty as flower in May have linger about you for your own sake . and since he be one whom you can never marry , you will believe he . there be an argument in favor of some other man . but do not   give yourself for a meal to a minotaur like Bult . I shall go now and pack . I shall make my excuse to Mrs. Arrowpoint . " Klesmer rise as he end , and walk quickly toward the door .

 " it would be too hard impossible you could not carry it through . I be not worth what you would have to encounter . I will not accept the sacrifice . it would be think a mťsalliance for you and I should be liable to the bad accusation . "

 the decisive word have be speak : there be no doubt concern the end will by each : there only remain the way of arrive at it , and Catherine determine to take the straight possible . she go to her father and mother in the library , and tell they that she have promise to marry Klesmer . 

 Mrs. Arrowpoint 's state of mind be pitiable . Imagine Jean Jacques , after his essay on the corrupting influence of the art , wake up among child of nature who have no idea of grill the raw bone they offer he for breakfast with the primitive flint knife ; or Saint Just , after fervidly denounce all recognition of pre - eminence , receive a vote of thank for the unbroken mediocrity of his speech , which warrant the dull patriot in deliver themselves at equal length . something of the same sort befell the authoress of " Tasso , " when what she have safely demand of the dead Leonora be enact by her own Catherine . it be hard for we to live up to our own eloquence , and keep pace with our wing word , while we be tread the solid earth and be liable to heavy dining . besides , it have long be understand that the propriety of literature be not those of practical life . Mrs. Arrowpoint naturally wish for the good of everything . she not only like to feel herself at a high level of literary sentiment than the lady with whom she associate ; she wish not to be behind they in any point of social consideration . while Klesmer be see in the light of a patronize musician , his peculiarity be picturesque and acceptable : but to see he by a sudden flash in the light of her son - in - law give she a burn sense of what the world would say . and the poor lady have be use to represent her Catherine as a model of excellence . 

 under the first shock she forget everything but her anger , and snatch at any phrase that would serve as a weapon .

 the father take his cigar from his mouth , and rise to the occasion by say , " this will never do , Cath . " 

 " do ! " cry Mrs. Arrowpoint ; " who in their sens ever think it would do ? you might as well say poisoning and strangling will not do . it be a comedy you have get up , Catherine . else you be mad . "

" every one else will say that for you . you will be a public fable . every one will say that you must have make an offer to a man who have be pay to come to the house who be nobody know what a gypsy , a Jew , a mere bubble of the earth .  " Mr. Arrowpoint , will you sit by and hear this without speak ? "

 " I do not   deny that , " say Catherine , get cold in proportion to her mother 's heat . " but one may say very true thing and apply they falsely . People can easily take the sacred word duty as a name for what they desire any one else to do . " 

 " your parent 's desire make no duty for you , then ? " 

 " yes , within reason . but before I give up the happiness of my life " 

 " Catherine , Catherine , it will not be your happiness , " say Mrs. 
 Arrowpoint , in her most raven - like tone . 
 " well , what seem to I my happiness before I give it up , I must see some well reason than the wish that I should marry a nobleman , or a man who vote with a party that he may be turn into a nobleman . I feel at liberty to marry the man I love and think worthy , unless some high duty forbid . " 

 " and so it do , Catherine , though you be blind and can not see it . 
 it be a woman 's duty not to lower herself . you be lower yourself . 
 Mr. Arrowpoint , will you tell your daughter what be her duty ? "

 " I really can not   see what that have to do with it , papa . the land of England have often pass into the hand of foreigner dutch soldier , son of foreign woman of bad character : if our land be sell to - morrow it would very likely pass into the hand of some foreign merchant on ' Change . it be in everybody 's mouth that successful swindler may buy up half the land in the country . how can I stem that tide ? "

 " I can not   see any public good concern here , papa , " say Catherine . " why be it to be expect of any heiress that she should carry the property gain in trade into the hand of a certain class ? that seem to be a ridiculous mishmash of superannuated custom and false ambition . I should call it a public evil . People have well make a new sort of public good by change their ambition . " 

 " that be mere sophistry , Catherine , " say Mrs. Arrowpoint . " because you do not   wish to marry a nobleman , you be not oblige to marry a mountebank or a charlatan . " 

 " I can not understand the application of such word , mamma . " 

 " no , I dare say not , " rejoin Mrs. Arrowpoint , with significant scorn . " you have get to a pitch at which we be not likely to understand each other . "

 " it certainly will not be do , " say Mrs. Arrowpoint , imperiously . 
 " where be the man ? let he be fetch . " 
 " I can not fetch he to be insult , " say Catherine . " nothing will be achieve by that . "

 " certainly ; if it be so , I should wish he to know it . " 

 " then you have well fetch he . " 

 Catherine only go into the music - room and say , " come . " she feel no need to prepare Klesmer .

 " you have take a base advantage of our confidence , " burst in Mrs. Arrowpoint , unable to carry out her purpose and leave the burden of speech to her husband .

 Klesmer . " my engagement be to she . "

 " he have well not count on our relenting , " say Mrs. Arrowpoint , whose manner suffer from that impunity in insult which have be reckon among the privilege of woman .

I be able to maintain your daughter , and I ask for no change in my life but her companionship . " 

 " you will leave the house , however , " say Mrs. Arrowpoint . 

 " I go at once , " say Klesmer , bow and quit the room . 

 " let there be no misunderstanding , mamma , " say Catherine ; " I consider myself engage to Herr Klesmer , and I intend to marry he . " 

 the mother turn her head away and wave her hand in sign of dismissal .
